topics.title: Cytoskeletal organization and dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT742 - CELL SIGNALING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cytoskeletal organization and dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

The **cytoskeleton** is a dynamic, three-dimensional network of interlinking protein filaments present throughout the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells. It provides **structural support (scaffolding)**, enables **intracellular transport**, mediates **cell motility**, and plays a central role in **signal transduction pathways** by physically relaying mechanical and biochemical cues from the plasma membrane to the nucleus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Verbatim):**
> *The cytoskeleton is a complex, ever-remodeling polymerized protein matrix composed of three principal filament systems — microfilaments (actin), intermediate filaments, and microtubules — whose assembly, disassembly, and interaction with motor proteins is tightly regulated by cellular signaling cascades.*

The three principal filament systems are characterized by their **diameter**, **subunit composition**, and **polarity**:

| Filament Type | Diameter | Subunit | Polarity | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Microfilament (MF) | **7 nm** | G-actin (42 kDa) | Yes (+/−) | Cell shape, motility, cytokinesis |
| Intermediate Filament (IF) | **8–12 nm** | Diverse (e.g., keratin, vimentin) | No (non-polar) | Mechanical resilience |
| Microtubule (MT) | **25 nm** | α/β-tubulin (50 kDa each) | Yes (+/−) | Transport, mitosis, organelle positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of **PEBBT742 (Cell Signaling)**, the cytoskeleton is NOT studied as a passive scaffold. The emphasis is on how signaling molecules (Rho-family GTPases, Ca²⁺, kinases, and phospholipids) actively *reorganize* the cytoskeleton to drive cell migration, axon guidance, and cytokinesis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Infrastructure" Model

Imagine the eukaryotic cell as a **bustling modern metropolis**:

- **Microtubules** are the **highways and metro lines** — long, straight, polarized tracks along which cargo (organelles, vesicles) is shuttled rapidly by motor proteins (kinesin, dynein).
- **Microfilaments (Actin)** are the **local streets and pedestrian walkways** — short, branched networks just beneath the membrane, enabling amoeboid movement, lamellipodia extension, and muscle contraction.
- **Intermediate Filaments** are the **steel girders and concrete rebar** of a skyscraper — they don't move things, but they provide **tensile strength**, anchoring the nucleus and resisting mechanical shear.
- **Motor proteins** (myosin, kinesin, dynein) are the **delivery trucks and trains** that walk along these tracks using **ATP as fuel**.

Just as a city's traffic flow is regulated by signal lights and control rooms, **signaling molecules** (Rho, Rac, Cdc42) act as the "traffic controllers" of the cytoskeleton, deciding when to build a highway, dismantle a street, or reroute a truck.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **biophysical constants** are routinely used in cytoskeletal studies:

- **Treadmilling rate of actin in vivo:** ~**0.1–1 μm/min**
- **Microtubule dynamic instability catastrophe frequency:** ~**0.01–0.1 s⁻¹**
- **Critical concentration (Cc) of G-actin:** ~**0.1 μM** (plus end), ~**0.6 μM** (minus end)
- **Critical concentration of tubulin:** ~**10–15 μM**
- **Step size of kinesin (per ATP hydrolyzed):** **8 nm** (≈ tubulin dimer length)
- **Step size of myosin V:** **~36 nm** (≈ actin monomer repeat of 13 subunits × 2.75 nm)
- **GTP hydrolysis rate on β-tubulin after polymerization:** **t₁/₂ ≈ several minutes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microfilament (Actin) Organization and Dynamics

Actin exists in two forms:
- **G-actin (globular, monomeric)** — the soluble, ATP-bound form
- **F-actin (filamentous, polymeric)** — the assembled double-helical filament

**Polymerization Reaction (KTU Key Equation):**

$$
n\,\text{G-actin-ATP} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{off}]{k_{on}}\; \text{F-actin} + n\,\text{P}_i
$$

Where:
- $k_{on}$ = association rate constant (M⁻¹s⁻¹)
- $k_{off}$ = dissociation rate constant (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **Treadmilling Phenomenon:** Because the two ends of an actin filament have different affinities for G-actin (the "barbed" + end binds tighter than the "pointed" − end), actin filaments undergo **treadmilling** — simultaneous addition at the (+) end and loss at the (−) end, while the *average filament length remains constant*. ATP hydrolysis on the actin monomer (after incorporation) creates an **ADP-actin "cap"** at the (−) end that destabilizes it.

**Rho-Family GTPase Regulation (Cell Signaling Context):**

| GTPase | Cytoskeletal Target | Cellular Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **RhoA** | Rho-kinase → Myosin Light Chain Phosphorylation | Stress fibers, focal adhesions |
| **Rac1** | WAVE / Arp2/3 complex | Lamellipodia (membrane ruffles) |
| **Cdc42** | WASP / N-WASP / Arp2/3 | Filopodia (spike-like protrusions) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** *Rho → Stress Fibers*, *Rac → Lamellipodia*, *Cdc42 → Filopodia*. This is a board-exam favorite. Memorize it as the **"RLC mnemonic" (Rho-LC, Rac-Lam, Cdc42-Filo)**.

### 2.2 Microtubule Organization and Dynamics

Microtubules are hollow, cylindrical polymers made of **α/β-tubulin heterodimers** arranged in **13 protofilaments**, with a characteristic **25 nm outer diameter** and **15 nm inner lumen**.

**Dynamic Instability** (Mitchison & Kirschner, 1984 concept):

Microtubules stochastically switch between two states:
- **Growth phase** — GTP-tubulin adds to the (+) end faster than hydrolysis
- **Catastrophe** — sudden depolymerization when the **GTP cap is lost**
- **Rescue** — regrowth resumes if a new GTP cap is established

> [!NOTE]
> **GTP Cap Model (Critical for KTU):** The β-tubulin subunit binds **GTP** before polymerization. Once incorporated, the GTP is **hydrolyzed to GDP** with a half-life of several minutes. As long as a "cap" of GTP-tubulin remains at the growing (+) end, the microtubule is stable. Loss of this cap (e.g., by dilution or slow addition) triggers **catastrophe**.

**Microtubule Organizing Centers (MTOCs):**
- **Centrosome** — the principal MTOC in animal interphase cells
- **Basal body** — MTOC for cilia and flagella
- **Spindle poles** — MTOCs during mitosis

### 2.3 Intermediate Filament Organization

Unlike actin and tubulin, intermediate filaments are **non-polar**, lack nucleotide-binding/hydrolysis dynamics, and provide purely **mechanical/structural** support.

**Hierarchical Assembly:**

$$
\text{Monomer} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Dimer (coiled-coil)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Tetramer} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Unit-length filament (ULF)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Mature 10-nm filament}
$$

**Five Major IF Classes:**

| Class | Members | Tissue Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| I & II | Acidic & basic keratins | Epithelia (tonofilaments) |
| III | Vimentin, desmin, GFAP, peripherin | Mesenchymal, muscle, glia, neurons |
| IV | Neurofilaments (NF-L, NF-M, NF-H), α-internexin | Neurons |
| V | Lamins (A, B, C) | Nuclear envelope (all eukaryotes) |
| VI | Nestin | Neural stem cells |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** *Lamins* are the only intermediate filaments located in the **nucleus**, forming the **nuclear lamina** that supports the inner nuclear membrane. Mutations in *LMNA* (lamin A gene) cause **progeria (Hutchinson-Gilford syndrome)** — a classic KTU essay question.

### 2.4 Motor Proteins and Cargo Transport

| Motor | Filament | Direction | Cargo Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Myosin II** | Actin | Toward (+) (barbed) end | Actomyosin contractile ring |
| **Myosin V** | Actin | Toward (+) end | Vesicles, melanosomes |
| **Kinesin-1 (conventional)** | Microtubule | Toward (+) end | Anterograde axonal transport |
| **Kinesin-14 (Ncd)** | Microtubule | Toward (−) end | Spindle pole focusing |
| **Dynein (cytoplasmic)** | Microtubule | Toward (−) end | Retrograde axonal transport |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All vertical bars (absolute value) are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Polymerization rate | $\dfrac{dL}{dt} = k_{on}[C] - k_{off}$ | Rate of filament elongation (nm/s) |
| Critical concentration | $C_c = \dfrac{k_{off}}{k_{on}}$ | Free subunit conc. at steady state (M) |
| Treadmilling flux | $J = k_{on}(C - C_c^{+}) = k_{off}(C_c^{-} - C)$ | Subunits/s translocating through filament |
| Diffusion (Einstein) | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r}$ | Free vesicle diffusion coeff. (m²/s) |
| Stall force (motor) | $F_{stall} = \dfrac{P_{hydrolysis}}{v_{unloaded}}$ | Max opposing force (pN) |
| Stokes viscous drag | $F_{drag} = 6 \pi \eta r v$ | Drag on spherical cargo (pN) |
| Thermal energy (kT) | $k_B T \approx 4.1\ \text{pN·nm at 25°C}$ | Scale of molecular forces |
| Catch-bond lifetime | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{F}{F_{\beta}}\right)$ | Bond lifetime vs. applied force |
| Slip-bond lifetime | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{F}{F_{\beta}}\right)$ | Lifetime decreases with force |
| Microtubule persistence length | $L_p \approx 1\text{–}6\ \text{mm}$ | Bending stiffness benchmark |
| Actin persistence length | $L_p \approx 10\text{–}20\ \mu\text{m}$ | More flexible than MT |
| Intermediate filament $L_p$ | $L_p \approx 0.3\text{–}1\ \mu\text{m}$ | Most flexible (yet strongest in tension) |

### 2.6 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Cytoskeletal principles are foundational in:
- **Biomedical engineering** — designing **microfluidic cell-migration assays** for drug screening.
- **Cancer therapeutics** — drugs like **Taxol (paclitaxel)** hyper-stabilize microtubules, while **vinblastine** depolymerizes them, halting mitosis in rapidly dividing tumor cells.
- **Tissue engineering** — engineering **substrate stiffness** to direct stem-cell differentiation via **mechanotransduction** (YAP/TAZ signaling).
- **Neuroscience** — understanding **axonal transport defects** in Alzheimer's, ALS, and Huntington's disease.
- **Cytoskeletal drug design** — **Latrunculin A** (sequesters G-actin), **Cytochalasin D** (caps (+) ends), **Jasplakinolide** (stabilizes F-actin), **Nocodazole** (depolymerizes MTs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Treadmilling Steady-State Flux

**Step 1 — Define the net rate of monomer addition at each end.**

The net elongation rate (in subunits per second) at each filament end follows first-order kinetics:

$$
\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)_{+} = k_{on}^{+}[C] - k_{off}^{+}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)_{-} = k_{on}^{-}[C] - k_{off}^{-}
$$

Where $[C]$ is the free G-actin concentration.

**Step 2 — At steady state for treadmilling, the (+) end grows and the (−) end shrinks at the same rate.**

Define the treadmilling flux $J$ such that the (+) elongation flux equals the (−) depolymerization flux (with sign convention):

$$
J = k_{on}^{+}[C] - k_{off}^{+} \;=\; k_{off}^{-} - k_{on}^{-}[C]
$$

**Step 3 — Express the free concentration in terms of the two critical concentrations.**

The critical concentration is defined as the $[C]$ at which the rate at a given end equals zero:
$C_c^{+} = k_{off}^{+}/k_{on}^{+}$ and $C_c^{-} = k_{off}^{-}/k_{on}^{-}$.

Substituting these into the flux equation:

$$
J = k_{on}^{+}\bigl([C] - C_c^{+}\bigr) \;=\; k_{on}^{-}\bigl(C_c^{-} - [C]\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the steady-state concentration $[C]_{ss}$.**

Equating the two expressions and solving for $[C]$:

$$
k_{on}^{+}[C] - k_{on}^{+}C_c^{+} \;=\; k_{on}^{-}C_c^{-} - k_{on}^{-}[C]
$$

$$
[C]\bigl(k_{on}^{+} + k_{on}^{-}\bigr) \;=\; k_{on}^{+}C_c^{+} + k_{on}^{-}C_c^{-}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;[C]_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{on}^{+}C_c^{+} + k_{on}^{-}C_c^{-}}{k_{on}^{+} + k_{on}^{-}}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug $[C]_{ss}$ back into the flux equation to obtain the treadmilling rate.**

The final result, expressed in terms of $C_c^{+}$ and $C_c^{-}$, is:

$$
\boxed{\;J_{treadmill} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{on}^{+}\,k_{on}^{-}}{k_{on}^{+} + k_{on}^{-}}\;\bigl(C_c^{-} - C_c^{+}\bigr)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Treadmilling occurs **only** if $C_c^{-} > C_c^{+}$, i.e., the (−) end has a *higher* critical concentration than the (+) end. This is precisely the case for actin: $C_c^{+} \approx 0.1\ \mu\text{M}$ and $C_c^{-} \approx 0.6\ \mu\text{M}$. The factor $(C_c^{-} - C_c^{+})$ is the **thermodynamic driving force** of treadmilling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Microtubule Dynamic Instability Catastrophe Frequency

**Step 1 — Model the GTP cap as a stochastic cap of $n$ GTP-tubulin subunits.**

The cap can be lost by:
- A single depolymerization event that exposes GDP-tubulin: rate $\approx v_{shrinking}$ (subunits/s).
- "Aging" of GTP to GDP on individual subunits: rate $\approx k_{hyd} = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}$.

**Step 2 — Catastrophe is triggered when the cap size $n$ falls to zero.**

The mean lifetime of the cap (and hence the mean time to catastrophe, $T_{cat}$) depends on the **growth velocity** $v_g$ and the hydrolysis rate $k_{hyd}$:

$$
\boxed{\;T_{cat} \;\approx\; \dfrac{n_{cap}}{v_g \cdot k_{hyd}\;\cdot\;\tau_{lag}}\;}
$$

Where $n_{cap}$ is the size of a minimal stable GTP cap (typically **2–4 layers** of GTP-tubulin) and $\tau_{lag}$ is the lag time for GDP exposure.

**Step 3 — Catastrophe frequency is the inverse of the mean time to catastrophe:**

$$
\boxed{\;f_{cat} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{cat}} \;\approx\; v_g \cdot k_{hyd}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition (KTU):** *Faster-growing microtubules paradoxically have HIGHER catastrophe frequencies*. This is because the elongation is so rapid that the hydrolysis machinery cannot "keep up" — the cap becomes thinner, eventually vanishing. This is the molecular basis of the famous **"search-and-capture"** mechanism by which microtubules find kinetochores during mitosis.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Filament Systems

| Parameter | Microfilament (Actin) | Intermediate Filament | Microtubule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Subunit mass** | **42 kDa** | 40–200 kDa (varies) | **50 kDa** (α + β tubulin) |
| **Diameter** | **7 nm** | **8–12 nm** | **25 nm** |
| **Polarity** | Yes (+/−, barbed/pointed) | **No** | Yes (+/−) |
| **Nucleotide use** | **ATP** → ADP + Pi | None (no NTP) | **GTP** → GDP + Pi |
| **Dynamics** | Treadmilling | Static (no exchange) | **Dynamic instability** |
| **Motor partner** | Myosin superfamily | None (no motors) | Kinesin, dynein |
| **Severing enzyme** | Gelsolin, cofilin | None dedicated | Katanin, spastin |
| **Stabilizing drug** | Jasplakinolide | — | **Taxol (paclitaxel)** |
| **Destabilizing drug** | Latrunculin, Cytochalasin D | — | Nocodazole, colchicine, vinblastine |
| **Key signaling regulator** | Rho-GTPases | Phosphorylation (e.g., by PKC, MAPK) | Aurora kinases, OP18/stathmin |
| **Persistence length** | ~10–20 μm | ~0.3–1 μm | ~1–6 mm |
| **Cell signaling role** | Morphology, mechanical force | Stress-sensing, mechanotransduction | Mitosis, polarity, transport |

---

### 3.4 Quick Numerical Example — Actin Treadmilling Rate

Given: $k_{on}^{+} = 11.6\ \mu\text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, $C_c^{+} = 0.10\ \mu\text{M}$, $C_c^{-} = 0.60\ \mu\text{M}$, free actin $[C] = 0.30\ \mu\text{M}$.

**Net (+) end growth rate:**

$$
\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)_{+} = k_{on}^{+}\bigl([C] - C_c^{+}\bigr) = 11.6 \times (0.30 - 0.10) = 2.32\ \text{subunits/s}
$$

**Net (−) end shrinkage rate:**

$$
\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)_{-} = k_{on}^{-}\bigl(C_c^{-} - [C]\bigr)
$$

Assuming $k_{on}^{-} \approx 1.3\ \mu\text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ (literature value):

$$
\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)_{-} = 1.3 \times (0.60 - 0.30) = 0.39\ \text{subunits/s}
$$

Since these are not equal, the system is **not at steady state**. To reach true treadmilling, $[C]$ must equal the steady-state value computed in §3.1:

$$
[C]_{ss} = \dfrac{(11.6)(0.10) + (1.3)(0.60)}{11.6 + 1.3} = \dfrac{1.16 + 0.78}{12.9} \approx 0.15\ \mu\text{M}
$$

At $[C] = 0.15\ \mu\text{M}$, both ends turn over at the same rate $J \approx 0.6$ subunits/s, and the filament treadmills in place.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Filament Hierarchical Comparison (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
    Factin["F-ACTIN FILAMENT 7 nm double helix"]:::polymer
    Myosin["MYOSIN II MOTOR walks toward plus end"]:::motor

    Tubulin["ALPHA BETA TUBULIN HETERODIMER 50 kDa GTP bound"]:::monomer

    IFmon["IF MONOMER central rod flanked by globular heads"]:::monomer
    IFfil["INTERMEDIATE FILAMENT 8 to 12 nm ROPE LIKE"]:::polymer
    Lam["LAMIN IF anchors inner nuclear membrane"]:::regulator

    Gactin -->|Polymerization ATP hydrolysis| Factin
    Factin <-->|Treadmilling dynamics| Gactin
    Myosin --> Factin

    Tubulin -->|GTP cap dependent| MT

    Dynein --> MT

    Factin -->|Signals| SignalOut[("CELL SIGNALING OUTPUT Migration Shape Force")]

```

### 4.2 Microtubule Dynamic Instability — GTP Cap State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> Growth
    Growth --> Growth : GTP tubulin adds to plus end faster than hydrolysis
    Growth --> Catastrophe : GTP cap lost hydrolysis overtakes polymerization
    Catastrophe --> Shrinking : Rapid depolymerization of GDP tubulin
    Shrinking --> Rescue : New GTP cap established or plus end stabilised
    Rescue --> Growth : Polymerization resumes
    Shrinking --> [*] : Complete depolymerization catastrophe full
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "treadmilling" (actin) with "dynamic instability" (microtubules) — they are mechanistically distinct. Treadmilling requires *unequal* critical concentrations; dynamic instability requires a *GTP cap*.
> 2. **Do NOT state** that intermediate filaments have polarity — they are non-polar.
> 3. **Do NOT misattribute** motors: *Kinesin = (+)*, *Dynein = (−)*, *Myosin = actin only*.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse** MTOC locations: *Centrosome = interphase animal cell*, *Basal body = cilium base*, *Spindle poles = mitotic*.
> 5. In Part B (14-mark) answers, **always include a labeled diagram** — it carries ~2–3 marks even if your prose is incomplete.

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between G-actin and F-actin. Add a note on the role of ATP in actin polymerization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | G-actin (Globular) | F-actin (Filamentous) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical state | Soluble monomer | Insoluble polymer |
| Diameter | ~5 nm (single molecule) | **7 nm** (filament) |
| Nucleotide bound | **ATP** | Mostly **ADP + Pi** (after hydrolysis) |
| Polarity | N/A | Has **barbed (+)** and **pointed (−)** ends |
| Function | Pool for polymerization | Structural/mechanical scaffold |

**ATP role (1 mark):** G-actin binds ATP before incorporation. After polymerization, ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP + Pi, which **destabilizes the filament interior** and promotes **treadmilling** by lowering the affinity of subunits at the (−) end.

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**What is the GTP cap model? How does it explain microtubule catastrophe and rescue?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **GTP cap model** proposes that microtubule stability is determined by a small **"cap" of GTP-bound β-tubulin subunits** at the growing (+) end.
- As long as the cap exists, the filament is **stable** and grows.
- Once the cap is lost (GTP hydrolyzed to GDP faster than new GTP-tubulin is added), the **exposed GDP-tubulin** is unstable → rapid depolymerization = **catastrophe**.
- If a **new GTP cap** is established before complete disassembly → regrowth = **rescue**.

This stochastic switching between growth and shrinkage is termed **dynamic instability**.

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> Choose **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the structural organization of the three principal cytoskeletal filament systems. Highlight differences in subunit composition, polarity, nucleotide usage, and associated motor proteins. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **treadmilling** in actin filaments with a derivation of the steady-state flux equation. How is this distinct from microtubule dynamic instability? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Structural Organization of the Three Filament Systems (7 marks)**

**Microfilaments (Actin filaments):**
- Composed of **G-actin monomers** (42 kDa) polymerized into a **right-handed double helix**.
- Filament diameter: **7 nm**.
- **Polarity:** Distinct (+) barbed end (fast-growing) and (−) pointed end.
- Nucleotide: **ATP** binds monomer; hydrolyzed to ADP + Pi post-polymerization.
- Motors: **Myosin superfamily** (e.g., Myosin II for contraction, Myosin V for vesicle transport).
- Functions: Cell shape, cytokinesis, lamellipodia, filopodia, muscle contraction.

**Intermediate Filaments:**
- Composed of **diverse, tissue-specific subunits** (keratins, vimentin, lamins, neurofilaments).
- Diameter: **8–12 nm** (intermediate between actin and MT).
- **Non-polar** — no intrinsic structural directionality.
- **No nucleotide binding/hydrolysis** — purely mechanical role.
- **No associated motor proteins.**
- Functions: Tensile strength, nuclear lamina (lamins), desmosomes/hemidesmosomes anchoring.

**Microtubules:**
- Composed of **α/β-tubulin heterodimers** (50 kDa each) arranged in **13 protofilaments** forming a hollow cylinder.
- Diameter: **25 nm** outer, 15 nm lumen.
- **Polarity:** (+) end grows faster; (−) end anchored at MTOC.
- Nucleotide: **GTP** (β-tubulin), hydrolyzed to GDP post-polymerization.
- Motors: **Kinesin** (anterograde, +) and **Dynein** (retrograde, −).
- Functions: Mitosis, intracellular transport, cell polarity, cilia/flagella.

**Comparative Diagram (Essential, 1 mark):**

A labeled schematic showing all three filaments side-by-side with their diameters and polarity (refer to §4.1 of these notes).

---

**(b) Actin Treadmilling and Its Distinction from Dynamic Instability (7 marks)**

**Treadmilling (3 marks):**
- Steady-state condition in which actin filament length remains **constant**, but G-actin subunits continuously **add at the (+) end** and **dissociate at the (−) end**.
- Driven by **differential critical concentrations**: $C_c^{-} > C_c^{+}$.
- ATP hydrolysis in the filament interior converts F-actin-ATP → F-actin-ADP, weakening the (−) end.
- Mathematical expression: $J = \dfrac{k_{on}^{+}\,k_{on}^{-}}{k_{on}^{+} + k_{on}^{-}}\,(C_c^{-} - C_c^{+})$

**Derivation Outline (2 marks):**
1. Rate at (+) end: $r_{+} = k_{on}^{+}[C] - k_{off}^{+}$
2. Rate at (−) end: $r_{-} = k_{on}^{-}[C] - k_{off}^{-}$
3. Steady state: $r_{+} = -r_{-}$ (or growth flux = shrinkage flux)
4. Solve for $[C]_{ss}$: $C_{ss} = \dfrac{k_{on}^{+}C_c^{+} + k_{on}^{-}C_c^{-}}{k_{on}^{+} + k_{on}^{-}}$
5. Substituting back yields the treadmilling flux equation.

**Distinction from Dynamic Instability (2 marks):**

| Feature | Actin Treadmilling | MT Dynamic Instability |
|---|---|---|
| Filament | Actin (7 nm) | Tubulin (25 nm) |
| Ends involved | Both (+) and (−) | Primarily (+) end |
| End behavior | (+) grows, (−) shrinks *simultaneously* | Single end switches between growth and shrinkage |
| Energy basis | ATP hydrolysis in filament | **GTP cap** presence/absence |
| Visual outcome | Length-constant filament flux | Length-fluctuating, stochastic switching |
| Catastrophe/rescue | Not applicable | **Yes** — key feature |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the structural organization of **microtubules** in detail. Explain **dynamic instability** with reference to the **GTP cap model** and discuss how **kinesin and dynein** motors achieve directional cargo transport. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Rho-family GTPases (RhoA, Rac1, Cdc42)** in cytoskeletal remodeling during cell migration. Illustrate with appropriate signaling cascades. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Microtubule Organization, Dynamic Instability, and Motor-Based Transport (7 marks)**

**Microtubule Structure (2 marks):**
- Hollow cylinder, **25 nm** outer diameter, **15 nm** lumen.
- Wall made of **13 parallel protofilaments** of alternating **α- and β-tubulin** heterodimers.
- **Plus (+) end** — fast-growing, β-tubulin exposed.
- **Minus (−) end** — slow-growing, α-tubulin exposed, anchored at MTOC.
- GTP binds β-tubulin; GDP remains on α-tubulin (non-exchangeable).

**Dynamic Instability and GTP Cap (3 marks):**
- Microtubules exhibit **stochastic switching** between **growth** and **shrinkage** phases.
- Newly added β-tubulin carries **GTP** → forms a stabilizing **GTP cap** at the (+) end.
- **GTP hydrolysis** to GDP lags slightly behind polymerization; the cap is "consumed" as new subunits add.
- **Catastrophe**: When the cap is lost, GDP-tubulin is exposed → rapid depolymerization (curling protofilaments).
- **Rescue**: If a new GTP cap reforms → polymerization resumes.
- Frequency: $f_{cat} \approx v_g \cdot k_{hyd}$ — faster growth paradoxically increases catastrophe.
- Biological role: **search-and-capture** of kinetochores during mitosis.

**Motor-Based Transport (2 marks):**
- **Kinesin-1 (conventional kinesin)**: Two-headed motor, walks **hand-over-hand** toward the (+) end, takes **8-nm steps per ATP**, delivers cargo in **anterograde axonal transport**.
- **Cytoplasmic dynein**: Larger, multi-subunit complex, walks toward the (−) end, performs **retrograde axonal transport**.
- Directionality ensures polarized cargo flux in neurons and mitotic spindle assembly.

---

**(b) Rho-GTPase Signaling in Cytoskeletal Remodeling (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Rho-family GTPases are **molecular switches** that cycle between inactive **GDP-bound** and active **GTP-bound** states. They are master regulators of actin dynamics and cell migration downstream of integrins, growth-factor receptors, and mechanical cues.

**The Trio — RhoA, Rac1, Cdc42 (4 marks):**

| GTPase | Downstream Effector | Cytoskeletal Output | Cellular Phenotype |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RhoA** | **ROCK (Rho-kinase)** → MLC phosphorylation; mDia formin | Contractile **stress fibers**, focal adhesions | Cell body contraction, tail retraction |
| **Rac1** | **WAVE** → **Arp2/3 complex** (branched actin nucleation) | **Lamellipodia** — broad sheet-like membrane protrusions | Forward protrusive force during migration |
| **Cdc42** | **WASP / N-WASP** → **Arp2/3 complex** (linear actin bundles) | **Filopodia** — thin, finger-like sensory protrusions | Direction sensing, axon guidance |

**Signaling Cascade — Integrin → Rho (2 marks):**
1. **ECM ligand** (e.g., fibronectin, collagen) binds **integrin** receptor.
2. Integrin clustering recruits **FAK (Focal Adhesion Kinase)** and **Src**.
3. FAK/Src activate **GEFs** (Guanine nucleotide Exchange Factors) — e.g., p115-RhoGEF, Vav (for Rac), intersectin (for Cdc42).
4. GEFs load **GTP** onto Rho/Rac/Cdc42.
5. Active GTPases bind downstream effectors (ROCK, WAVE, WASP) → cytoskeletal remodeling.
6. **GAPs** (GTPase-Activating Proteins) hydrolyze GTP to GDP → signal termination.

**Net Migration Outcome:** Coordinated *Rac-driven protrusion → Cdc42-driven polarization → Rho-driven retraction* produces directed cell migration (a process called the **"mesenchymal migration cycle"**).

> Refer to the **Mermaid flowchart in §4.3** for the integrated signaling schematic.

---

### 📝 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Cram Sheet — Module 1: Cytoskeleton**

- ✅ Cytoskeleton = **3 filament systems**: **A**ctin (7 nm), **I**ntermediate (8–12 nm), **M**icrotubule (25 nm) → *Mnemonic: "AIM"*.
- ✅ Actin uses **ATP**; Microtubule uses **GTP**; IF uses **neither**.
- ✅ Only actin and MT are **polar**; IF is **non-polar**.
- ✅ **Treadmilling** = actin (unequal $C_c$ at two ends).
- ✅ **Dynamic instability** = microtubule (GTP cap loss = catastrophe; new cap = rescue).
- ✅ **Kinesin** = (+) end; **Dynein** = (−) end; **Myosin** = actin only.
- ✅ **MTOC**: centrosome (interphase), basal body (cilia), spindle pole (mitosis).
- ✅ **Rho → Stress Fibers**; **Rac → Lamellipodia**; **Cdc42 → Filopodia** (RLC mnemonic).
- ✅ IFs lack motors and nucleotide dynamics; purely structural.
- ✅ **Lamins** = nuclear IFs; LMNA mutation → **progeria**.
- ✅ **Taxol** stabilizes MT; **Nocodazole/Colchicine/Vinblastine** depolymerize MT.
- ✅ **Latrunculin** sequesters G-actin; **Cytochalasin D** caps (+) end; **Jasplakinolide** stabilizes F-actin.
- ✅ Treadmilling flux equation: $J = \dfrac{k_{on}^{+}\,k_{on}^{-}}{k_{on}^{+} + k_{on}^{-}}\,(C_c^{-} - C_c^{+})$.
- ✅ Catastrophe frequency: $f_{cat} \approx v_g \cdot k_{hyd}$ (faster growth → higher catastrophe).
- ✅ Signaling regulators: **Rho-GTPases**, **ROCK**, **WAVE/WASP/Arp2/3**, **Aurora kinases**, **Stathmin/OP18**.
- ✅ Drawing a **labeled comparative diagram** in 14-mark answers = free 2–3 marks.
- ✅ Always state the **direction of motor movement** when asked about transport.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell adhesion and extracellular matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT742 - CELL SIGNALING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cell adhesion and extracellular matrix # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Cell Adhesion — Formal Definition **Cell adhesion** is the tightly regulated process by which cells physically attach to neighbouring cells (cell–cell adhesion) and to the surrounding **extracellular matrix (ECM)** (cell–matrix adhesion) through specialised transmembrane glycoproteins called **Cell Adhesion Molecules (CAMs)**. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this is the *foundation layer* upon which all higher-order tissue architecture, morphogenesis, immune surveillance, wound healing, and signal transduction events are built. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > *"Cell adhesion molecules (Cadherins, Integrins, Selectins, Ig-CAMs); Cell–cell junctions (Tight, Adherens, Gap, Desmosomes); Extracellular matrix – composition, structure and receptors."* ## 1.2 Extracellular Matrix (ECM) — Formal Definition The **extracellular matrix** is a complex, hydrated, three-dimensional macromolecular network secreted by cells that fills the intercellular space. It is composed of: - **Fibrous proteins** — collagen, elastin - **Proteoglycans** — aggrecan, perlecan - **Multi-adhesive glycoproteins** — fibronectin, laminin The ECM provides **structural scaffolding, biochemical cues, and mechanical signals** that dictate cell fate, polarity, migration, proliferation, and differentiation. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Brick-and-Mortar City" Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — Imagine your body as a city:** > - **Cells** = the *buildings/houses* (residents). > - **Cell Adhesion Molecules (CAMs)** = the *door-locks, hinges, bolts, and intercom systems* that lock a house to its neighbour and to the pavement. > - **Junctions** = the *sealed seams* between adjacent walls (tight junctions = silicone seal, desmosomes = rivets, gap junctions = intercom, adherens = welded plates). > - **Extracellular Matrix** = the *land, soil, tar roads, and concrete pavements* on which the houses stand, plus the *cables and pipes (fibronectin/laminin)* that carry signals. > > Without CAMs, buildings would float apart. Without ECM, the city would have no roads or foundations. **Cancer metastasis** is literally a *building ripping itself free, walking out, and re-planting elsewhere* — driven by loss of these locks and pavements. ## 1.4 Why Cell Adhesion & ECM Matter in KTU Examinations | Clinical / Biological Phenomenon | Adhesion / ECM Component Involved | |---|---| | Embryonic morphogenesis | Cadherins + ECM remodelling | | Cancer metastasis | Loss of E-cadherin, integrin switching | | Leukocyte extravasation | Selectins → Integrins → IgCAMs cascade | | Wound healing | Fibronectin, collagen deposition | | Muscular dystrophy | Dystrophin–dystroglycan complex | | Skin blistering (pemphigus) | Desmosomal cadherin autoantibodies | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Major Families of Cell Adhesion Molecules (CAMs) Cell adhesion is mediated by **four principal superfamilies** of CAMs. Each operates via distinct molecular mechanisms and serves distinct biological roles. ### 2.1.1 Cadherins — The "Classical" Calcium-Dependent CAMs - **Discovery/Key Property:** Named for **calcium-dependent adhesion**. Classical cadherins (E-, N-, P-, VE-cadherin) mediate **homophilic, homotypic** adhesion (a cadherin on cell A binds the *same type* of cadherin on cell B). - **Mechanism:** Five extracellular *cadherin repeats* (EC1–EC5) bind Ca²⁺ rigidly, enabling *trans*-dimerization with cadherins on the apposing cell. - **Cytoplasmic tail:** Binds **catenins** (β-catenin / p120-catenin) → links to the **actin cytoskeleton** via α-catenin. - **Function:** Forms the **adherens junction** core; determines tissue boundary identity ("adhesion code"). - **Clinical relevance:** E-cadherin loss → epithelial–mesenchymal transition (**EMT**) in cancer. ### 2.1.2 Integrins — The "Bidirectional" Heterodimeric Receptors - **Structure:** Heterodimeric **αβ** transmembrane glycoproteins (18 α-subunits, 8 β-subunits → ~24 combinations in humans). - **Key Property:** **Bidirectional signalling**: - *Inside-out*: intracellular talin/kindlin binding activates the extracellular ligand-binding site. - *Outside-in*: ECM ligand binding triggers FAK → Src → MAPK/PI3K cascades. - **Ligands:** **RGD** (Arg-Gly-Asp) motif in fibronectin, laminin, collagen, vitronectin. - **Function:** Cell–matrix adhesion, focal adhesions, mechanotransduction, platelet aggregation. ### 2.1.3 Selectins — The "Rolling" Lectin-Domain CAMs - **Structure:** Type-I transmembrane proteins with an N-terminal **C-type lectin domain**, an EGF-like domain, and short consensus repeats (SCRs). - **Members:** **L-selectin** (leukocytes), **P-selectin** (platelets/endothelium), **E-selectin** (endothelium). - **Ligands:** Sialyl-Lewis-X (sLeX) and related **glycan** epitopes on mucin-like glycoproteins. - **Function:** Mediate the **rolling** step of the leukocyte adhesion cascade in inflammation. ### 2.1.4 Immunoglobulin-CAMs (IgCAMs) — The "Antibody-Like" CAMs - **Structure:** Extracellular region contains multiple **Ig-like domains** (β-sandwich folds) and often fibronectin type-III (FN3) repeats. - **Members:** **NCAM** (neural), **VCAM-1** (vascular), **ICAM-1/2/3**, **L1-CAM**, **ALCAM**. - **Binding mode:** Mostly **heterophilic** (e.g., ICAM-1 binds LFA-1 integrin) and sometimes homophilic. - **Function:** Nervous system wiring, leukocyte firm adhesion, immune synapse formation. ## 2.2 Cell–Cell Junctions — The Five Junctional Complexes Epithelial cells assemble a **junctional complex** (apical → basal): 1. **Tight Junction (Zonula Occludens)** — Claudins + Occludin; seals paracellular space; fence function. 2. **Adherens Junction (Zonula Adherens)** — Cadherin–catenin complex + actin ring; mechanical adhesion. 3. **Desmosome (Macula Adherens)** — Desmocollin/Desmoglein + plakoglobin/desmoplakin + **intermediate filaments (keratin)**. 4. **Gap Junction (Nexus)** — **Connexin 43** hexameric *connexons*; allows passage of ions & small metabolites (<1 kDa). 5. **Hemidesmosome** — Integrin α6β4 + plectin; anchors basal surface to basement membrane via **intermediate filaments**. ## 2.3 Extracellular Matrix (ECM) — Compositional Deep-Dive ### 2.3.1 Collagens — The Most Abundant Animal Protein - **28 types** identified; types I, II, III, IV are most common. - **Structure:** Triple helix of three α-chains (Gly-X-Y repeats; X often Pro, Y often Hyp). - **Fibrillar collagens** (I, II, III): form banded fibrils → tensile strength (tendon, bone, skin, cartilage). - **Network-forming** (IV): forms the **basement membrane** sheet. - **FACIT collagens** (IX, XII): link fibrils to other ECM components. ### 2.3.2 Elastin — The Recoil Element - Hydrophobic, cross-linked via **desmosine** and **isodesmosine** (unique lysine-derived crosslinks). - Provides **elasticity** in arteries, lungs, skin, ligaments. ### 2.3.3 Proteoglycans & Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) - **GAGs:** Unbranched polysaccharides — **hyaluronic acid (HA)**, chondroitin sulfate, heparan sulfate, keratan sulfate, dermatan sulfate. - **Proteoglycans:** Core protein + GAG chains. Examples: **aggrecan** (cartilage), **perlecan** (basement membrane), **syndecan** (transmembrane). - HA is unique — **non-sulfated, not attached to a core protein**, binds CD44 receptor. ### 2.3.4 Multi-Adhesive Glycoproteins - **Fibronectin (FN):** Dimer of two subunits linked by disulfide bonds; contains **RGD** motif for integrin α5β1; promotes cell migration. - **Laminin:** Cross-shaped heterotrimer (α, β, γ chains); major component of **basal lamina**; binds integrin α6β1, α6β4 and dystroglycan. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table > [!NOTE] > **Master Table — Memorise for KTU ESE:** | Adhesion Molecule / Component | Family | Ca²⁺-Dependent? | Ligand / Partner | Cytoskeletal Linkage | Key Biological Role | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | E-cadherin | Classical cadherin | Yes | Homophilic (E-cadherin) | Actin (via β-catenin/α-catenin) | Epithelial sheet integrity | | N-cadherin | Classical cadherin | Yes | Homophilic | Actin | Neuronal / cardiac adhesion | | Desmoglein/Desmocollin | Desmosomal cadherin | Yes | Homophilic | Intermediate filaments (via plakoglobin/desmoplakin) | Strong tissue adhesion | | Integrin α5β1 | Integrin | No (divalent cations: Mg²⁺, Mn²⁺) | Fibronectin (RGD) | Actin (focal adhesion) | Cell–matrix adhesion | | Integrin α6β4 | Integrin | No | Laminin | Intermediate filaments (hemidesmosome) | Basal anchorage | | P-selectin | Selectin | Yes (for lectin domain) | Sialyl-Lewis-X (PSGL-1) | None (rolling receptor) | Leukocyte rolling | | ICAM-1 | IgCAM | No | LFA-1 (αLβ2 integrin) | None (immune synapse) | Firm leukocyte adhesion | | NCAM | IgCAM | No | Homophilic (Ig domains) | None / spectrin | Neuronal outgrowth | | Connexin 43 | Gap junction protein | No | Connexin 43 (other cell) | None | Intercellular communication | | Collagen I | ECM fibrous | No (triple helix) | Integrin α1β1, α2β1 | N/A (ECM) | Tensile strength | | Fibronectin | ECM glycoprotein | No | Integrin α5β1 (RGD), collagen, fibrin | N/A | Cell migration, wound healing | | Laminin | ECM glycoprotein | No | Integrin α6β1, α6β4, dystroglycan | N/A | Basal lamina scaffold | | Aggrecan | Proteoglycan | No | Hyaluronan, link protein | N/A | Cartilage hydration, resilience | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic — "CaM-IS"** for the four CAM families: > **Ca**dherins → **M** (IgCAMs) → **I**ntegrins → **S**electins. > Cadherins and Selectins are **Ca²⁺-dependent**; Integrins are **Cation-dependent (Mg²⁺/Mn²⁺)**; IgCAMs are **Ca²⁺-independent**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility - **Tissue engineering scaffolds** — Decellularised ECM (e.g., urinary bladder matrix) is used as surgical grafts. - **3D hydrogels** — Hyaluronic acid and collagen gels are used as *in-vitro* tumour models. - **Biomaterial surface functionalisation** — Coating titanium implants with **RGD peptides** enhances osseointegration via integrin engagement. - **Drug-delivery targeting** — L-selectin ligands are exploited to target drugs to inflamed endothelium. - **Anti-cancer therapeutics** — Antibodies/inhibitors against αvβ3 and αvβ5 integrins (e.g., **cilengitide**) target tumour angiogenesis. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Throughs & Adhesion Mechanism Cascade ## 3.1 Mechanism — Integrin "Outside-In" Signalling Cascade **Scenario:** A fibroblast encounters a fibronectin-rich matrix wound site. The α5β1 integrin engages the RGD motif of fibronectin. The full cascade is enumerated step-by-step below. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Ligand binding:} \quad & \alpha5\beta1 \text{ integrin binds RGD motif of fibronectin.} \\ & \text{Clustering of integrins forms a } \textbf{focal adhesion (FA)} \text{ precursor.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2 — Talin unfolding:} \quad & \text{Intracellular PIP}_2 \text{ binds talin head domain,} \\ & \text{unmasking FERM domain → binds } \beta\text{-integrin cytoplasmic tail.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3 — Kindlin cooperation:} \quad & \text{Kindlin-2 binds the } \beta\text{-integrin NxxY motif.} \\ & \text{Talin + Kindlin jointly stabilise the } \textbf{active (high-affinity)} \text{ conformation.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4 — FAK auto-phosphorylation:} \quad & \text{Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) autophosphorylates at Tyr397.} \\ & \text{Src SH2 domain binds pY397 → activates Src kinase.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 5 — Scaffold assembly:} \quad & \text{pY397-FAK recruits } \text{GRB2-SOS, p85(PI3K), Cas, Paxillin.} \\ & \text{Actin polymerisation via Rac1/Cdc42/RhoA GTPases.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 6 — Downstream pathways:} \quad & \text{RAF} \xrightarrow{} \text{MEK} \xrightarrow{} \text{ERK MAPK pathway} \rightarrow \text{proliferation/gene expression.} \\ & \text{PI3K} \xrightarrow{} \text{PIP}_3 \xrightarrow{} \text{AKT} \rightarrow \text{survival/migration.} \\ & \text{ILK (Integrin-Linked Kinase)} \rightarrow \text{AKT, GSK-3}\beta \rightarrow \text{Wnt crosstalk.} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Net Outcome:** ECM rigidity + integrin engagement → FAK activation → proliferative and survival signalling. This is the molecular basis of **anoikis resistance** in metastatic cancer cells. ## 3.2 Mechanism — Cadherin-Mediated Adherens Junction Assembly $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Synthesis:} \quad & \text{E-cadherin is synthesised in the ER, glycosylated in Golgi,} \\ & \text{delivered to the basolateral membrane in vesicles.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2 — cis-Dimerization:} \quad & \text{On the same cell surface, two E-cadherins pair laterally (} cis \text{) via EC1 domains.} \\ & \text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ ions rigidify the extracellular region.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3 — trans-Interaction:} \quad & \text{The } cis \text{-dimer on cell A engages the } cis \text{-dimer on cell B (} trans \text{) via EC1 "strand swap".} \\ & \text{This forms the } \textbf{adhesive zipper} \text{ between cells.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4 — Catenin recruitment:} \quad & \text{Cytoplasmic tail of E-cadherin binds } \beta\text{-catenin (or p120-catenin).} \\ & \beta\text{-catenin recruits } \alpha\text{-catenin.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 5 — Actin linkage:} \quad & \alpha\text{-catenin binds F-actin directly (and via vinculin, EPLIN). \\ & \text{Mechanoresponsive reinforcement under tension.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 6 — Signalling crosstalk:} \quad & \beta\text{-catenin (free pool) \rightarrow Wnt signalling → TCF/LEF transcription.} \\ & \text{E-cadherin sequesters } \beta\text{-catenin at membrane → downregulates Wnt.} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write that "β-catenin directly binds F-actin." **This is incorrect.** β-catenin binds E-cadherin and α-catenin; α-catenin binds actin. (Loss of β-catenin → *no* adherens junction even though E-cadherin is present.) ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The Leukocyte Adhesion Cascade The four-step paradigm of leukocyte extravasation, a classic KTU Module 1 application: | Step | Adhesion Molecule | Action | Duration / Affinity | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Tethering & Rolling | **E-selectin / P-selectin** binds sLeX on PSGL-1 | Slows leukocyte; reversible | Low affinity, high on-rate | | 2. Activation | **Chemokines** (e.g., IL-8, CXCL12) → GPCR signalling | Activates leukocyte integrins | Seconds | | 3. Firm Adhesion | **LFA-1 (αLβ2)** binds **ICAM-1/2**; **VLA-4 (α4β1)** binds **VCAM-1** | Arrest on endothelium | High affinity | | 4. Transmigration (Diapedesis) | **PECAM-1 (CD31)**, **JAM-A**, **CD99** | Crawls through endothelial junctions | Step-wise | ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Basement Membrane Composition The basement membrane (basal lamina) is a specialised sheet-like ECM. Its four principal components: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Basement Membrane} &= \text{Collagen IV (network scaffold)} \\ &\quad + \text{Laminin (cell-binding, polymerises independently)} \\ &\quad + \text{Nidogen/Entactin (cross-links laminin and collagen IV)} \\ &\quad + \text{Perlecan (heparan sulfate proteoglycan — charge barrier, FGF storage)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic — "CL-EN-P"** for basement membrane: **C**ollagen IV, **L**aminin, **E**ntactin/nidogen, N → e**N**tactin, **P**erlecan. ## 3.5 Crosstalk Matrix — How Adhesion Molecules Influence Signalling Pathways | Adhesion Component | Pathway Activated | Biological Consequence | |---|---|---| | Integrin α5β1 + fibronectin | FAK → ERK MAPK | Proliferation, fibronectin matrix assembly | | Integrin α6β4 + laminin | FAK → PI3K → AKT | Survival, hemidesmosome disassembly in migration | | E-cadherin (membrane-bound) | Sequesters β-catenin | Inhibits Wnt-driven proliferation | | Loss of E-cadherin | Free β-catenin → Wnt | EMT, cancer invasion | | NCAM homophilic binding | FGF receptor clustering | Neurite outgrowth | | Syndecan-4 | PKCα, RhoG | Focal adhesion turnover | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic — Junctional Complex of an Epithelial Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB LUMEN["Apical Lumen"] subgraph APICAL_REGION["Apical Region"] TJ["Tight Junction
Claudin + Occludin + ZO-1
Paracellular Seal"] AJ["Adherens Junction
E-cadherin + beta-catenin + alpha-catenin
Actin Belt"] end subgraph LATERAL_REGION["Lateral Region"] DS["Desmosome
Desmoglein + Desmocollin + Plakoglobin
Keratin IF Linkage"] GJ["Gap Junction
Connexin 43 Connexon
Ion / Metabolite Channel"] end subgraph BASAL_REGION["Basal Region"] HD["Hemidesmosome
Integrin alpha6beta4 + Plectin
Keratin IF Linkage"] FA["Focal Adhesion
Integrin alpha5beta1 + Talin + FAK + Paxillin
Actin Stress Fibres"] end ECM["Extracellular Matrix
Collagen I/III + Fibronectin
and Basal Lamina
Collagen IV + Laminin + Nidogen + Perlecan"] ACTIN["F-actin Cytoskeleton"] IF["Intermediate Filament Cytoskeleton"] LUMEN --> TJ --> AJ --> DS --> GJ --> HD --> FA --> ECM AJ --- ACTIN FA --- ACTIN DS --- IF HD --- IF ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Integrin "Outside-In" Signalling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR ECM["ECM Ligand
Fibronectin / Laminin
RGD Motif"] INT["Integrin alpha-beta heterodimer
Extracellular Ligand-binding Head
Transmembrane region
Cytoplasmic Tail"] TALIN["Talin Head
FERM Domain
Binds beta-cytoplasmic tail"] KIND["Kindlin-2
Binds NxxY motif"] FAK["FAK
Autophosphorylation Tyr397"] SRC["Src Family Kinase
SH2 binds pY397"] GRB["GRB2-SOS Adaptor"] PAX["Paxillin Scaffold"] PI3K["p85 Regulatory Subunit of PI3K"] CAS["p130Cas Scaffold"] RAF["RAF"] MEK["MEK"] ERK["ERK MAPK"] AKT["AKT / PKB"] RAC["Rac1 / Cdc42 / RhoA GTPases"] ACTIN["F-actin Polymerisation
Lamellipodia / Filopodia"] NUCL["Nucleus
Gene Expression
Proliferation / Survival / Migration"] ECM -- RGD binding --> INT INT -- clustered --> FAK INT -- binds --> TALIN INT -- binds --> KIND TALIN --> FAK KIND --> FAK FAK --> SRC SRC --> GRB SRC --> PAX SRC --> CAS GRB --> RAF RAF --> MEK MEK --> ERK ERK --> NUCL PAX --> PI3K PI3K --> AKT AKT --> NUCL CAS --> RAC RAC --> ACTIN ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Hierarchy of CAMs, Junctions, and ECM ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LEVEL1["Level 1 - Cell Adhesion Molecules"] CAD["Cadherins
Ca2+ dependent homophilic"] INT["Integrins
alpha-beta heterodimers
Bidirectional signalling"] SEL["Selectins
Lectin domain
Bind sLeX glycans"] IGC["IgCAMs
Ig-like domains
Mostly heterophilic"] end subgraph LEVEL2["Level 2 - Junctional Assemblies"] TJ["Tight Junction"] AJ["Adherens Junction"] DS["Desmosome"] GJ["Gap Junction"] HD["Hemidesmosome"] FA["Focal Adhesion"] end subgraph LEVEL3["Level 3 - ECM Macro-Architecture"] BL["Basal Lamina
Collagen IV + Laminin
Nidogen + Perlecan"] IC["Interstitial Matrix
Collagen I/III + Elastin
Fibronectin + Proteoglycans"] end subgraph LEVEL4["Level 4 - Tissue-Level Outcomes"] TI["Tissue Integrity
Barrier Function
Mechanical Strength"] SIG["Signal Transduction
Survival / Proliferation
Migration / Differentiation"] DIS["Disease States
Cancer Metastasis
Inflammation
Blistering Disorders"] end CAD --> AJ CAD --> DS INT --> HD INT --> FA SEL --> ROLL["Leukocyte Rolling
Inflammation"] IGC --> FA2["Firm Adhesion
Immune Synapse"] AJ --> TI DS --> TI TJ --> TI GJ --> SIG FA --> SIG HD --> TI BL --> TI BL --> SIG IC --> TI IC --> SIG TI --> DIS SIG --> DIS ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Leukocyte Adhesion Cascade (Step-wise Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1 - Tethering and Rolling
E-selectin and P-selectin on endothelium
bind sLeX on PSGL-1 of leukocyte
LOW affinity reversible"] S2["Step 2 - Chemokine Activation
IL-8 or CXCL12 on endothelium
binds GPCR on leukocyte
Rapid integrin conformational activation"] S3["Step 3 - Firm Adhesion
LFA-1 integrin alphaLbeta2
binds ICAM-1 and ICAM-2
and VLA-4 alpha4beta1 binds VCAM-1
HIGH affinity arrest"] S4["Step 4 - Transmigration
PECAM-1 CD31 and JAM-A and CD99
direct leukocyte through
endothelial junctions
Paracellular or Transcellular route"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 S1 -.low affinity.-> S3 S3 -.arrest.-> S4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU ESE — Module 1):** > - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks > - Part B: Internal choice between 2 questions × 14 marks each = 14 marks > - **Total Module 1 contribution to ESE: 20 marks** --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **"Differentiate between homophilic and heterophilic cell adhesion. Give one example of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Homophilic | Heterophilic | |---|---|---| | Definition | **Same adhesion molecule** on both apposing cells binds each other | **Different adhesion molecules** on the two apposing cells bind each other | | Specificity | High — drives tissue-specific sorting | Lower — enables transient cell–cell interactions | | Example 1 | E-cadherin on epithelial cell A binds E-cadherin on epithelial cell B | ICAM-1 (IgCAM) on endothelium binds LFA-1 (integrin αLβ2) on leukocyte | | Example 2 | NCAM–NCAM binding in neurons | L1-CAM binds αvβ3 integrin or other IgCAMs | | Cadherin behaviour | Classical cadherins are predominantly homophilic | — | | Functional role | Stable, long-term tissue integrity | Dynamic processes — immune synapse, migration | *(1.5 marks for definition + table; 1.5 marks for examples.)* ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **"List the four major families of cell adhesion molecules with one distinguishing feature of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Cadherins** — Ca²⁺-dependent homophilic adhesion; mediate adherens junctions and desmosomes. 2. **Integrins** — Heterodimeric αβ transmembrane receptors; mediate bidirectional signalling (inside-out & outside-in). 3. **Selectins** — Possess C-type lectin domain; bind sialyl-Lewis-X glycan epitopes; mediate leukocyte rolling. 4. **IgCAMs (Immunoglobulin superfamily CAMs)** — Have Ig-like domains; mostly heterophilic binding; e.g., ICAM-1, NCAM, VCAM-1. *(0.75 mark per family — naming + distinguishing feature.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]` **"Describe in detail the structure, classification, and biological functions of cadherins. Add a note on the role of E-cadherin loss in cancer metastasis."** #### Part (a) — Structure & Classification of Cadherins (7 marks) **1. Classical Cadherins (Type I):** - E-cadherin (CDH1) — epithelial - N-cadherin (CDH2) — neural, cardiac, mesenchymal - P-cadherin (CDH3) — placental, epidermal - VE-cadherin (CDH5) — vascular endothelial **2. Structure:** - Single-pass transmembrane glycoprotein (~720–880 aa). - **Extracellular region:** 5 *cadherin (EC) repeats* of ~110 aa each. - **Ca²⁺ binding sites** between EC repeats (3 Ca²⁺ per EC interface) — rigidifies the rod-like structure. - **Transmembrane domain.** - **Cytoplasmic tail** with two catenin-binding regions. **3. Cadherin–Catenin Complex (Adherens Junction Core):** - β-catenin (or γ-catenin/plakoglobin) binds the **C-terminal cytoplasmic tail**. - α-catenin binds β-catenin and links to **F-actin**. - p120-catenin binds the *juxtamembrane* cytoplasmic domain — stabilises cadherin at the membrane and regulates endocytosis. **4. Adhesion Mechanism (already detailed in §3.2):** - cis-dimerization on same cell, trans-interaction across cells. - Requires Ca²⁺ (chelatation by EGTA abolishes adhesion). **5. Desmosomal Cadherins (Type II / Non-classical):** - Desmogleins (DSG1–4) and Desmocollins (DSC1–3). - Link to **intermediate filaments** (keratin) via plakoglobin, plakophilin, and desmoplakin. **6. Other Cadherins:** - Protocadherins (largest subgroup, neuronal). - FAT cadherins, CELSR cadherins, calsyntenins. *(Model mark breakdown: 1.5 — types; 2 — structure; 2 — catenin complex; 1.5 — adhesion mechanism; 1 — desmosomal & others.)* #### Part (b) — E-cadherin Loss & Cancer Metastasis (7 marks) **1. E-cadherin — The "Suppressor" of Invasion:** - E-cadherin is the principal cadherin of **epithelial adherens junctions**. - It maintains epithelial sheet integrity, cell polarity, and contact inhibition of proliferation. **2. Mechanism of E-cadherin Loss (Multi-level):** - **Genetic:** *CDH1* gene mutations (germline → hereditary diffuse gastric cancer; somatic → lobular breast carcinoma). - **Epigenetic:** Promoter hypermethylation of *CDH1* (silencing). - **Transcriptional repression:** EMT-inducing transcription factors — **Snail, Slug, Zeb1, Zeb2, Twist** — bind E-boxes in the *CDH1* promoter and repress transcription. - **Post-translational:** Endocytosis and degradation of E-cadherin (Hakai-mediated ubiquitination, lysosomal targeting). **3. Consequence — Epithelial–Mesenchymal Transition (EMT):** - Loss of E-cadherin → loss of cell–cell adhesion. - Cells lose apical–basal polarity, become migratory and invasive. - Acquisition of mesenchymal markers (N-cadherin "cadherin switch", vimentin, fibronectin). - Free β-catenin translocates to nucleus → TCF/LEF-driven transcription (cyclin D1, c-Myc). **4. Metastatic Cascade:** - **Detachment** of tumour cells from primary site. - **Invasion** through basement membrane (matrix metalloproteinases cleave collagen IV). - **Intravasation** into lymphatics/blood vessels. - **Survival** in circulation (anoikis resistance via integrin/EGFR crosstalk). - **Extravasation** at distant site. - **Colonisation** (requires E-cadherin re-expression for metastatic outgrowth — "cadherin switch" reverse). **5. Therapeutic Implications:** - **Cdh1 restoration** strategies: demethylating agents, Snail siRNA, recombinant E-cadherin Fc fusion proteins. - β-catenin inhibitors (PRI-724) in clinical trials for Wnt-driven cancers. *(Model mark breakdown: 1.5 — suppressor concept; 2 — mechanism of loss; 2 — EMT & metastasis; 1.5 — therapeutic implications.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model]` **"Explain the composition and biological functions of the extracellular matrix. Discuss the role of integrins in cell–matrix adhesion and signal transduction."** #### Part (a) — Composition of ECM (7 marks) **1. Fibrous Proteins:** - **Collagens (28 types)** — most abundant animal protein (~30% of body protein). Type I (bone, tendon, skin), Type II (cartilage), Type III (reticular fibres), Type IV (basement membrane). Triple-helix of Gly-X-Y repeats. - **Elastin** — hydrophobic, cross-linked by desmosine/isodesmosine; provides elastic recoil (skin, arteries, lungs). **2. Proteoglycans & GAGs:** - **GAGs:** Hyaluronic acid (non-sulfated, not protein-linked), chondroitin sulfate, heparan sulfate, keratan sulfate, dermatan sulfate. - **Core proteoglycans:** Aggrecan (cartilage), perlecan (basement membrane), decorin, biglycan, syndecan (transmembrane). - Highly negatively charged → high water retention → **turgor pressure** in cartilage. **3. Multi-adhesive Glycoproteins:** - **Fibronectin** — dimer with RGD motif, binds integrins (α5β1), collagen, fibrin; essential for cell migration, wound healing. - **Laminin** — cross-shaped heterotrimer (α, β, γ); major basal lamina component; binds integrins (α6β1, α6β4), dystroglycan, nidogen. **4. Basal Lamina (Basement Membrane):** - Specialised sheet-like ECM underlying epithelia and surrounding muscle, fat, Schwann cells. - Composed of: **Collagen IV + Laminin + Nidogen/Entactin + Perlecan (HSPG)**. **5. ECM Degrading Enzymes:** - **Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs):** Zn²⁺-dependent endopeptidases; collagenases (MMP-1, -8, -13), gelatinases (MMP-2, -9), stromelysins (MMP-3, -10, -11). - **Tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs):** TIMP-1, -2, -3, -4. - **ADAMs / ADAMTS** families. *(Model mark breakdown: 1.5 — collagens; 1 — elastin; 1.5 — proteoglycans/GAGs; 1.5 — glycoproteins; 1.5 — basal lamina & MMPs.)* #### Part (b) — Integrins in Cell–Matrix Adhesion & Signalling (7 marks) **1. Integrin Structure:** - Heterodimeric αβ transmembrane receptors. - 18 α and 8 β subunits → 24 combinations in humans. - Each subunit has a large extracellular domain, single transmembrane helix, and short cytoplasmic tail (except β4 with ~1000 aa tail). - **Metal-ion dependent adhesion site (MIDAS)** in the β-subunit binds ligand in Mg²⁺/Mn²⁺-dependent manner. **2. RGD Motif Recognition:** - Discovered by Pierschbacher & Ruoslahti (1984). - Arg-Gly-Asp (RGD) is the canonical integrin-binding motif in fibronectin. - Synthetic RGD peptides competitively inhibit cell–matrix adhesion. **3. Inside-Out Signalling:** - Resting integrin adopts **bent, low-affinity** conformation. - Talin head binds β-cytoplasmic tail → triggers **ectodomain extension and separation of α/β legs** → high-affinity state. - Kindlin cooperates with talin. - Rap1–RIAM pathway regulates talin delivery to membrane. **4. Outside-In Signalling (Cascade in §3.1):** - ECM engagement → integrin clustering → **focal adhesion** formation. - FAK autophosphorylation at Y397 → Src recruitment → downstream MAPK/PI3K/AKT. - Mechanical linkage to actin via talin, vinculin, α-actinin, VASP. - **Mechanotransduction:** Stiff ECM → more force on talin → unfolding → vinculin recruitment → focal adhesion maturation. **5. Biological Consequences:** - **Cell migration** (lamellipodia, filopodia, focal adhesion turnover). - **Anchorage-dependent growth** (loss of adhesion → anoikis). - **Differentiation** (myoblast fusion requires integrin engagement). - **Wound healing, immune cell trafficking, platelet aggregation** (αIIbβ3). - **Cancer:** αvβ3 and αvβ5 in angiogenesis; α5β1 in fibronectin assembly. **6. Therapeutic Targeting of Integrins:** - **Abciximab, Eptifibatide, Tirofiban** — αIIbβ3 inhibitors for anti-platelet therapy. - **Vedolizumab, Natalizumab** — α4 integrin blockers for inflammatory bowel disease and multiple sclerosis. - **Cilengitide** — αvβ3/αvβ5 antagonist (anti-angiogenic, in cancer trials). *(Model mark breakdown: 1 — structure; 1 — RGD; 1.5 — inside-out; 1.5 — outside-in; 1 — biology; 1 — therapeutics.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Adhesion / ECM Questions:** > 1. **Conflating adherens junctions and desmosomes.** Adherens junctions = **cadherins + actin**; desmosomes = **desmoglein/desmocollin + intermediate filaments**. Mixing these costs ~2 marks. > 2. **Writing that "β-catenin binds F-actin directly."** Always interpose α-catenin. > 3. **Forgetting Ca²⁺ dependence** of cadherins and selectins — a classic 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Stating integrins are Ca²⁺-dependent** — they are **divalent cation-dependent (Mg²⁺/Mn²⁺)**. Marks lost for missing this. > 5. **Not distinguishing classical vs. desmosomal cadherins** when asked to "classify cadherins." > 6. **Omitting the cytoplasmic catenin complex** in the cadherin structure — the catenin linkage IS the functional link to the cytoskeleton. > 7. **Ignoring the "inside-out" mode** of integrins — many students describe only outside-in and lose 1–2 marks. > 8. **Failing to mention basal lamina components** (collagen IV, laminin, nidogen, perlecan) when describing ECM. > 9. **Confusing "homophilic" with "homotypic"** — they are not identical. Homophilic = same molecule; homotypic = same cell type. In the context of CAMs, "homophilic binding" is the standard term. > 10. **Missing the "RGD motif"** when describing integrin–fibronectin interaction — Ruoslahti's classical discovery, frequently asked. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read in the Last Hour Before the Exam:** - **Cell Adhesion** = process by which cells attach to each other (cell–cell) and to ECM (cell–matrix) via CAMs. - **Four CAM families** — **Cadherins, Integrins, Selectins, IgCAMs** (Mnemonic: **"CaM IS"**). - **Cadherins** — Ca²⁺-dependent, *homophilic*, classical (E/N/P/VE) and desmosomal (DSG/DSC). Link to **actin** (via catenins) or **intermediate filaments** (via plakoglobin/desmoplakin). - **Integrins** — αβ heterodimers, ~24 combinations. **Bidirectional** signalling. Bind **RGD** in fibronectin. Mg²⁺/Mn²⁺-dependent. - **Selectins** — L, P, E. Lectin domain binds **sialyl-Lewis-X**. Mediate **leukocyte rolling**. - **IgCAMs** — NCAM, ICAM-1, VCAM-1. Mostly **heterophilic**. Ig-like + FN3 domains. Firm adhesion & immune synapse. - **Junctions** — Tight (claudin/occludin, paracellular seal), Adherens (E-cadherin, actin belt), Desmosome (DSG/DSC, keratin IFs), Gap (connexon, ion/metabolite channel), Hemidesmosome (α6β4 integrin, keratin IFs). - **ECM components** — Collagens (28 types; I/II/III = fibrillar, IV = network), Elastin (desmosine crosslinks), Proteoglycans/GAGs (HA, aggrecan, perlecan), Glycoproteins (fibronectin, laminin). - **Basal Lamina** — "**CL-EN-P**" = **C**ollagen IV + **L**aminin + **E**ntactin/Nidogen + **P**erlecan. - **FAK pathway** — Integrin → FAK (pY397) → Src → GRB2-SOS → RAF → MEK → ERK → nucleus. - **E-cadherin loss** → EMT → cancer metastasis; mediated by Snail/Slug/Zeb/Twist and *CDH1* promoter methylation. - **Cadherin switch** — E-cadherin (epithelial) → N-cadherin (mesenchymal) during EMT. - **Anoikis** — apoptosis due to loss of anchorage; resisted in metastasis via integrin/EGFR crosstalk. - **MMPs** (Zn²⁺-dependent endopeptidases) + **TIMPs** (inhibitors) regulate ECM turnover. - **Leukocyte cascade** — 4 steps: **Tethering/Rolling** (Selectin–sLeX) → **Activation** (Chemokine–GPCR) → **Firm Adhesion** (LFA-1–ICAM, VLA-4–VCAM) → **Transmigration** (PECAM-1, JAM-A). - **Therapeutic antibodies** — Abciximab (αIIbβ3), Vedolizumab (α4β7), Natalizumab (α4), Cilengitide (αvβ3/αvβ5). - **RGD motif** = Arg-Gly-Asp; first identified in fibronectin (Pierschbacher & Ruoslahti, 1984). - **Connexin 43** = major gap junction protein; forms hexameric *connexons*; allows <1 kDa molecules through.

topics.title: Cell division cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT742 - CELL SIGNALING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cell division cycle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Cell Division Cycle** (also known as the **Cell Cycle**) is the highly regulated, sequential series of events that takes place in a cell leading to its division and the duplication of its DNA to produce two daughter cells. According to the KTU 2024 Cell Signaling syllabus, it encompasses the tightly orchestrated progression through four distinct phases: **G1 (Gap 1)**, **S (Synthesis)**, **G2 (Gap 2)**, and **M (Mitosis)**, interspersed with regulatory **checkpoints** governed by cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs), cyclins, and the Anaphase-Promoting Complex/Cyclosome (APC/C). In molecular terms, the cell cycle is a **bistable biochemical oscillator** driven by the periodic synthesis and degradation of regulatory proteins (cyclins) and the activation/inactivation of their catalytic partners (CDKs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The cell division cycle forms the foundational context for understanding how extracellular signals, growth factors, and checkpoint proteins regulate proliferation — a key bridge into the signal transduction cascades covered later in the course. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cell as a Smart Factory" Imagine a cell as a **highly automated biscuit factory** that must produce a perfect copy of itself: - **G1 Phase (Growth 1):** The factory reviews its inventory, grows in size, reads the "orders" (growth factor signals), and decides whether to proceed with production. It checks: *"Do we have enough raw materials? Are market conditions right?"* — this is the **Restriction Point / G1 Checkpoint**. - **S Phase (Synthesis):** The factory duplicates its blueprints (DNA replication) so that each new factory will get a complete copy. - **G2 Phase (Growth 2):** Quality control. The factory checks: *"Are the blueprints accurate? Is all machinery ready?"* — this is the **G2/M Checkpoint**. - **M Phase (Mitosis):** The factory physically divides — first the blueprints are separated (mitosis), then the entire building splits into two (cytokinesis). If any check fails, the cycle halts — much like a factory's automated shutdown to prevent defective products. > [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Distinction:** > - **Interphase** = G1 + S + G2 (the "preparation and duplication" phase, ~90% of the cycle) > - **M Phase** = Mitosis + Cytokinesis (the "division" phase, ~10% of the cycle) > - **G0 Phase** = Quiescent state (cell exits the cycle and stops dividing) ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **Average human cell cycle duration:** ~**24 hours** (24 h) - **M phase duration:** ~**1 hour** (1 h) - **G1 duration:** variable (**11 h** typical) - **S phase duration:** ~**8 hours** (8 h) - **G2 duration:** ~**4 hours** (4 h) - **G0 duration:** Indefinite (terminally differentiated cells) - **Key protein families:** Cyclins (A, B, D, E), CDKs (CDK1, CDK2, CDK4, CDK6) - **Universal checkpoint genes:** p53, Rb, p21, p27, ATM, ATR, Chk1, Chk2 # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Phase-by-Phase Operational Breakdown ### **Phase 1: G1 (Gap 1) — "Decision Phase"** - **What happens:** Cell grows in size, synthesizes mRNA, proteins, and organelles. - **Molecular events:** Cyclin D binds **CDK4/6** → phosphorylates **Rb (Retinoblastoma protein)** → releases **E2F transcription factor** → drives transcription of S-phase genes. - **Critical decision point:** The **Restriction Point (R)** in late G1. Past this point, the cell is committed to division even without external growth signals. - **Key regulators:** Growth factors (EGF, PDGF), Myc, Rb, E2F, Cyclin D-CDK4/6. ### **Phase 2: S Phase (Synthesis) — "DNA Duplication Phase"** - **What happens:** Complete replication of the nuclear DNA (3.2 billion base pairs in human genome). - **Molecular events:** DNA polymerases (Pol δ, Pol ε) replicate; **Cyclin A-CDK2** activates replication origins; Origin Recognition Complex (ORC) licensed. - **Key regulators:** Cyclin A-CDK2, Cdc6, Cdt1, MCM complex, PCNA. ### **Phase 3: G2 (Gap 2) — "Pre-mitotic Quality Control"** - **What happens:** Cell prepares for mitosis, synthesizes mitotic spindle proteins, repairs any DNA damage. - **Molecular events:** **Cyclin B-CDK1 (MPF)** accumulates in the cytoplasm; DNA damage checkpoint active (ATM/ATR → Chk1/Chk2 → inhibit Cdc25). - **Key regulators:** Cyclin B-CDK1 (MPF = Maturation Promoting Factor), Cdc25 phosphatase, Plk1. ### **Phase 4: M Phase (Mitosis) — "Division Phase"** - **Sub-phases:** Prophase → Prometaphase → Metaphase → Anaphase → Telophase → Cytokinesis. - **Molecular events:** - Nuclear envelope breakdown (NEB) via Cyclin B-CDK1 phosphorylation of lamins. - Chromosome alignment at metaphase plate. - **Anaphase onset** triggered by **APC/C** (Anaphase-Promoting Complex/Cyclosome) — ubiquitinates securin → activates separase → cleaves cohesin. - Cyclin B degradation by APC/C exits mitosis. - **Key regulators:** APC/C (with Cdc20), securin, separase, cohesin. ## 2.2 The Three Major Checkpoints (Engineering Control Theory View) | Checkpoint | Location | Triggered By | Outcome if Failed | Analogy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **G1/S Checkpoint (Restriction Point)** | Late G1 | DNA damage, nutrient deprivation, lack of growth factors | DNA replicated with damage → mutations | "Order approval checkpoint" | | **G2/M Checkpoint** | G2/M boundary | Unreplicated or damaged DNA | Daughter cells with broken chromosomes | "Quality inspection before shipping" | | **Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC)** | Metaphase → Anaphase | Improper kinetochore-microtubule attachment | Aneuploidy (wrong chromosome number) | "Final count before packaging" | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In bioengineering, cell cycle checkpoints are modeled as **nonlinear feedback control systems**. The bistable switch behavior of CDK1 activation is exploited in synthetic biology circuits and cancer therapeutics (CDK4/6 inhibitors like palbociclib). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Relationship | Description | |---|---|---| | Cyclin accumulation rate | $\frac{d[Cyclin]}{dt} = k_{syn} - k_{deg}\cdot[Cyclin]$ | First-order synthesis/degradation kinetics | | CDK1 active fraction | $[CDK1_{active}] = \frac{[Total\ CDK1]}{1 + (K_d/[Cyclin\ B])^n}$ | Hill function, n = cooperativity (~4-8) | | Michaelis-Menten (CDK phosphorylation) | $v = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Substrate phosphorylation by CDK | | Doubling time | $t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Exponential growth: $N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}$ | | Hill equation (gene regulation) | $f = \frac{[L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n}$ | Transcription factor binding to promoters | | Phosphorylation-dephosphorylation switch | $\frac{dX^*}{dt} = k_1(X_{tot} - X^*) - \frac{V_{max} X^*}{K_m + X^*}$ | Bistable switch model (Goldbeter) | | MPF activation threshold | $[Cyclin\ B]_{crit} = K_d \cdot \left(\frac{n}{n-1}\right)^{1/n}$ | Threshold for mitotic entry | | Ubiquitin-proteasome targeting | $\frac{d[Cyclin\ B]}{dt} = -k_{APC}[Cyclin\ B][APC/C^*]$ | APC/C-mediated degradation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Note for KTU 2024:** The cell cycle is modeled as a **bistable switch** by Goldbeter (1991) and Tyson & Novak (1993). These models demonstrate how positive feedback (e.g., Cdc25 activating CDK1, Wee1 inhibiting CDK1) creates an "all-or-none" transition between interphase and mitosis — a key concept connecting **systems biology** to **cell signaling**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cancer therapeutics:** CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib) exploit the G1/S checkpoint. - **Regenerative medicine:** Manipulating G0 exit to reprogram quiescent cells. - **Biomanufacturing:** Synchronizing cell cultures using double-thymidine block (S-phase arrest) for maximum bioproduct yield. - **Aging research:** Replicative senescence (Hayflick limit, ~**50 divisions**) is tied to telomere shortening detected at checkpoints. - **Systems biology:** Cell cycle ODE models are benchmark systems for testing numerical solvers in MATLAB/Simulink. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Goldbeter's Bistable Mitotic Switch (1979 Model) This derivation shows mathematically why the cell cycle behaves as a "switch" rather than a smooth gradient — a core concept in KTU Module 1. ### **Step 1: Define the variables** Let $X$ be the **active (phosphorylated) form of MPF (Cyclin B-CDK1)** and $X_T$ be the **total MPF**. The fraction of active MPF is: $$v = \frac{[X]}{[X_T]}$$ This is the "control variable" we wish to analyze. --- ### **Step 2: Activation by Cdc25 phosphatase** Cdc25 dephosphorylates inactive MPF to active MPF. Modeling with Michaelis-Menten kinetics: $$\text{Rate of activation} = \frac{V_{M1} \cdot v}{K_{m1} + v}$$ where $V_{M1}$ is the maximum rate and $K_{m1}$ is the Michaelis constant. --- ### **Step 3: Inactivation by Wee1 kinase** Wee1 phosphorylates active MPF to inactive MPF: $$\text{Rate of inactivation} = \frac{V_{M2} (1 - v)}{K_{m2} + (1 - v)}$$ --- ### **Step 4: Equate activation and inactivation for steady states** At steady state, $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$: $$\frac{V_{M1} \cdot v}{K_{m1} + v} = \frac{V_{M2} (1 - v)}{K_{m2} + (1 - v)}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{v(K_{m1} + v)}{K_{m1}} = \frac{V_{M1}}{V_{M2}} \cdot \frac{(1-v)[K_{m2} + (1-v)]}{K_{m2}}$$ --- ### **Step 5: Introduce the activity ratio and solve** Define $K = V_{M1}/V_{M2}$. Cross-multiplying yields a **cubic polynomial in v**: $$K \cdot v(K_{m1} + v) \cdot [K_{m2} + (1-v)] = (1-v) \cdot K_{m1} \cdot [K_{m2} + (1-v)]$$ After full expansion (substituting $K_{m1} = K_{m2} = K_m$ for simplicity): $$K \cdot v(K_m + v)(K_m + 1 - v) = (1-v) \cdot K_m \cdot (K_m + 1 - v)$$ --- ### **Step 6: Condition for bistability** This cubic equation has **three real roots** (one unstable, two stable) when the following condition is satisfied: $$K > \frac{(1 - 2K_m)^2}{4K_m(1-K_m)}$$ For typical parameter values ($K_m = 0.01$, $K = 10$), this condition is satisfied, confirming **bistability**. --- ### **Step 7: Biological interpretation** The **two stable steady states** correspond to: - **Low $v$:** Interphase (MPF inactive, cell growing) - **High $v$:** Mitosis (MPF active, cell dividing) The **unstable middle root** acts as a threshold — the cell "flips" between states when Cyclin B concentration crosses a critical value, explaining the abrupt entry into mitosis. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Doubling Time from Exponential Growth ### **Step 1: Cell population growth equation** $$\frac{dN}{dt} = k \cdot N(t)$$ where $N(t)$ is cell number and $k$ is the specific growth rate (h⁻¹). --- ### **Step 2: Solve the ODE** $$\int \frac{dN}{N} = \int k\,dt \implies \ln N = kt + C$$ Applying initial condition $N(0) = N_0$: $$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{kt}$$ --- ### **Step 3: Define doubling time** At $t = t_d$, $N(t_d) = 2N_0$: $$2N_0 = N_0 \cdot e^{k t_d}$$ $$2 = e^{k t_d}$$ --- ### **Step 4: Solve for $t_d$** $$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$$ --- ### **Step 5: Numerical example** For HeLa cells with $k = 0.035$ h⁻¹: $$t_d = \frac{0.693}{0.035} \approx 19.8 \text{ hours}$$ This matches experimental observations of ~20 h doubling time for HeLa cancer cells. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cyclin-CDK Pairs and Their Functions | Cyclin | Partner CDK | Active Phase | Primary Substrates | Outcome | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Cyclin D** (D1, D2, D3) | CDK4, CDK6 | G1 (early) | Rb protein | E2F release → S-phase entry | | **Cyclin E** | CDK2 | G1/S transition | Rb, p27, p21, Cdc6 | Initiation of DNA replication | | **Cyclin A** | CDK2 | S phase | Cdc6, MCM, E2F | DNA replication completion | | **Cyclin A** | CDK1 | G2 | Cyclin B, Cdc25, Plk1 | Mitotic entry preparation | | **Cyclin B** | CDK1 (Cdc2) | G2/M and M | Lamins, condensins, Golgi matrix | Nuclear envelope breakdown, chromosome condensation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** Remember the order — **D**EAD **B**oy (D for G1, E for G1/S, A for S/G2, B for M). --- ## 3.4 Quantitative Checkpoint Analysis The fraction of cells in each phase can be measured by **flow cytometry** (FACS) using DNA content: - **G1 cells:** DNA content = 2N → fluorescence intensity = 1 unit - **S cells:** DNA content = 2N → 4N (actively replicating) → 1 to 2 units - **G2/M cells:** DNA content = 4N → fluorescence intensity = 2 units The cell cycle phase distribution follows: $$P_i = \frac{N_i}{\sum_j N_j}$$ where $N_i$ is the count of cells in phase $i$. This is used in **synchronization experiments** and **drug screening** in biotech industries. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Cell Division Cycle Phases & Transitions ```mermaid graph TD A([G0 - Quiescent State]) -->|Growth Factor Signal| B[G1 Phase - Gap 1] B -->|Rb Phosphorylated
Cyclin D-CDK4/6 Active| C{G1/S Checkpoint
Restriction Point} C -->|Pass| D[S Phase - DNA Synthesis] C -->|Fail - p53 activation| A D -->|Cyclin A-CDK2 Active
DNA Replicated| E[G2 Phase - Gap 2] E -->|Cyclin B-CDK1 Accumulates| F{G2/M Checkpoint
DNA Damage Check} F -->|Pass - Cdc25 Active| G[M Phase - Mitosis] F -->|Fail - ATM/ATR| H[Cell Cycle Arrest
DNA Repair] H -->|Repair Complete| G G -->|Prophase| I[Chromosome Condensation] I -->|Prometaphase| J[Nuclear Envelope Breakdown] J -->|Metaphase| K[Spindle Assembly Checkpoint] K -->|Pass - APC/C Activated| L[Anaphase] K -->|Fail| M[Metaphase Arrest] L -->|Telophase| N[Cytokinesis] N --> O([Two Daughter Cells]) O -->|Continue Cycling| B O -->|Exit to G0| A style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style O fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px style F fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px style K fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cyclin-CDK Regulatory Network ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Extracellular GF[Growth Factors: EGF, PDGF, IGF] end subgraph G1_Phase_Regulation GF -->|Receptor Tyrosine Kinase| RAS[RAS GTPase] RAS --> RAF[RAF-MEK-ERK Cascade] RAF --> MYC[Myc Transcription Factor] MYC --> CycD[Cyclin D Synthesis] CycD --> CDK46[CDK4 and CDK6] CDK46 -->|Phosphorylate| RB[Rb Protein] RB -->|Released| E2F[E2F Transcription Factor] E2F --> SGenes[S-Phase Genes: Cyclin E, DNA Pol] end subgraph S_G2_Transition SGenes --> CycE[Cyclin E] CycE --> CDK2A[CDK2] CDK2A --> CycA[Cyclin A] CycA --> CDK2B[CDK2] end subgraph M_Phase_Regulation CycA --> CycB[Cyclin B] CycB --> CDK1[CDK1 / MPF] WEE1[Wee1 Kinase] -->|Inhibitory| CDK1 CDC25[Cdc25 Phosphatase] -->|Activating| CDK1 CDK1 -->|Positive Feedback| CDC25 CDK1 -->|Inhibitory Feedback| WEE1 end subgraph Mitotic_Exit CDK1 -->|Activates| APC[APC/C-Cdc20] APC -->|Ubiquitinates| CycB CycB -->|Degraded| Exit[Anaphase Onset and Mitotic Exit] end style GF fill:#fadbd8 style CDK1 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:3px style APC fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#239b56,stroke-width:2px style Exit fill:#d5f5e3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cell Cycle Control ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Input_Layer EXT[Extracellular Signals] INT[Intracellular Status
DNA Damage, Nutrients] end subgraph Sensor_Layer RTK[Receptor Tyrosine Kinases] DDR[DNA Damage Sensors
