5. **Resolution:** Once all kinetochores achieve proper bipolar attachment, Mad2 no longer generates the wait signal, MCC dissociates from APC/C, and APC/C becomes active.

6. **Failure of SAC (Clinical Significance):** When SAC components (Mad2, BubR1) are mutated or silenced, chromosomes segregate prematurely → **aneuploid daughter cells** (gained or lost chromosomes) → **genomic instability**, a hallmark of **colorectal, cervical, and breast cancers**. The phenomenon is also implicated in **Down syndrome (trisomy 21)** due to meiotic SAC failure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **role of p53 as the guardian of the genome** in cell cycle control. Explain how p53 activation leads to either **cell cycle arrest or apoptosis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of Wee1 kinase and Cdc25 phosphatase in regulating entry into mitosis**. How does the DNA damage response modulate this switch? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) p53 – Guardian of the Genome (7 marks)**

1. **p53 Discovery and Significance:** p53 is a **tumor suppressor transcription factor** encoded by the *TP53* gene, mutated in **>50% of all human cancers**. It is termed the "guardian of the genome" because of its central role in responding to DNA damage.

2. **Steady-State Regulation:** Under normal conditions, p53 is kept at low levels through **MDM2-mediated ubiquitination** and proteasomal degradation. MDM2 acts as a ubiquitin ligase that continuously tags p53 for destruction.

3. **Activation by DNA Damage:** Upon DNA double-strand breaks, the **ATM kinase** (and ATR for replication stress) is activated. ATM phosphorylates p53 at **Ser15** (and other residues), **disrupting the p53–MDM2 interaction**. Phosphorylation of MDM2 by ATM further inhibits its ligase activity.

4. **Stabilization and Tetramerization:** Stabilized p53 forms **tetramers** that bind to p53 response elements in DNA to transactivate target genes.

5. **Outcome 1 — Cell Cycle Arrest:** p53 transcribes **p21 (CDKN1A)**, a CKI that binds and inhibits **Cyclin E–CDK2** and **Cyclin A–CDK2**, causing **G1/S arrest**. This provides time for DNA repair machinery (nucleotide excision repair, base excision repair) to act.

6. **Outcome 2 — Apoptosis (if damage is irreparable):** p53 transcribes **BAX, PUMA, NOXA** (pro-apoptotic Bcl-2 family members). These translocate to mitochondria, oligomerize to form pores in the outer mitochondrial membrane, release **cytochrome c**, activate **caspase-9** → caspase-3 → **irreversible programmed cell death**.

7. **Clinical Correlation:** **Li-Fraumeni syndrome** (germline TP53 mutation) predisposes to multiple early-onset cancers.

**(b) Wee1 and Cdc25 in Mitosis Entry (7 marks)**

1. **The Mitotic Trigger:** Entry into mitosis requires activation of **Cyclin B–CDK1** (also called **M-phase Promoting Factor, MPF**). The critical regulatory step is the **phosphorylation status of CDK1 at Tyr-15** (within the kinase ATP-binding loop).

2. **Wee1 Kinase (Inhibitor):** Wee1 is a **nuclear kinase** that adds an **inhibitory phosphate** to **Tyr-15 of CDK1**, keeping Cyclin B–CDK1 in an **inactive (phosphorylated) state** during S and G2 phases. Wee1 activity prevents premature mitotic entry.

3. **Cdc25 Phosphatase (Activator):** Cdc25 (in mammals, Cdc25A, B, C) is a **dual-specificity phosphatase** that **removes the inhibitory phosphate from Tyr-15** (and Thr-14), thereby activating CDK1.

4. **Bistable Switch (Positive Feedback):** Once Cdc25 is partially activated, active CDK1 **phosphorylates and further activates Cdc25** (positive feedback). Simultaneously, CDK1 **phosphorylates and inhibits Wee1** (double-negative regulation). This produces a **bistable, all-or-none switch** that drives abrupt mitotic entry.

5. **DNA Damage Modulation:**
   - Upon DNA damage in G2, **ATR → Chk1** (S-phase) or **ATM → Chk2** (DSBs) pathway is activated.
   - Chk1/Chk2 **phosphorylate Cdc25** at Ser-216, creating a **14-3-3 binding site** that sequesters Cdc25 in the cytoplasm, away from nuclear CDK1.
   - Chk1/Chk2 also **stabilize Wee1**.
   - Net result: **CDK1 remains Tyr-15 phosphorylated** → **G2/M arrest**, giving the cell time to repair DNA.

6. **Therapeutic Implication:** **Wee1 inhibitors** (e.g., Adavosertib/AZD1775) are in clinical trials as cancer therapeutics — they **force cancer cells with DNA damage into premature mitosis**, leading to **mitotic catastrophe** and cell death.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cyclin D–CDK4/6 with Cyclin E–CDK2 functions.** Cyclin D–CDK4/6 does **monophosphorylation of Rb**; Cyclin E–CDK2 does the **hyperphosphorylation**. Mixing them up = 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Writing "p53 causes apoptosis" without the molecular cascade** (ATM → p53-P → BAX → cytochrome c → caspase-9). Examiners expect the full pathway.
> 3. **Forgetting that Wee1 is a KINASE (inhibitor) and Cdc25 is a PHOSPHATASE (activator).** This is a high-frequency error worth 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning the Restriction Point concept explicitly** in G1/S questions — losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Diagrams without labels** lose 2 marks reserved for schematic clarity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cell Cycle Control**

- ✅ **Cyclin-CDK pairing** is the core engine: D-CDK4/6 (G1), E-CDK2 (G1/S), A-CDK2 (S), A-CDK1 (G2), B-CDK1 (M).
- ✅ **Rb phosphorylation** is the molecular switch for Restriction Point crossing — E2F is the downstream transcription factor.
- ✅ **Three kinase families regulate CDK1 Tyr-15:** Wee1 (inhibitory kinase), Cdc25 (activating phosphatase), Chk1/Chk2 (modulators during damage).
- ✅ **Four checkpoints** must be remembered: **G1/S, Intra-S, G2/M, Spindle Assembly (SAC)**.
- ✅ **SAC components:** Mad2, BubR1, Bub3, Cdc20 — inhibit APC/C until all kinetochores are attached.
- ✅ **APC/C$^Cdc20$** ubiquitinates **securin** and **cyclin B** → anaphase onset and mitotic exit.
- ✅ **CKIs:** p21 (p53-induced, broad), p27 (G1/S), p16 (INK4, CDK4/6-specific).
- ✅ **p53 decision tree:** Low damage → p21 → arrest → repair. High damage → BAX/PUMA → apoptosis.
- ✅ **Cancer drug targets:** CDK4/6 inhibitors (Palbociclib), Wee1 inhibitors (Adavosertib), MDM2 antagonists.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for SAC proteins: "Mad Bub**" — **Mad2, BubR1, Bub3**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signaling defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT742 - CELL SIGNALING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Signaling defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signaling Defects** are defined as alterations (genetic, epigenetic, or post-translational) in the molecular components of cellular signal transduction pathways — including ligands, receptors, second messengers, adaptor proteins, kinases, phosphatases, and transcription factors — that lead to aberrant signal initiation, propagation, amplification, or termination, ultimately resulting in loss of cellular homeostasis, uncontrolled proliferation, metabolic dysfunction, immune dysregulation, or cell death.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance aligned with **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** under NEP 2020, signaling defects are studied as the **molecular pathology basis of diseases**, linking bench-level biochemistry to bedside clinical phenotypes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a city's traffic light system:
- **Receptors** are the sensors (cameras, pressure plates) detecting vehicle flow.
- **Kinases & second messengers** are the control units sending signals to the lights.
- **Transcription factors** are the traffic managers who decide "go" or "stop" based on the signal.
- **Phosphatases & negative regulators** are the auto-reset mechanisms that return lights to default.

A **signaling defect** is like a faulty camera stuck on "always see cars" (constitutive activation) or a traffic light bulb that never turns green (loss-of-function), or a manager who issues "go" signals even when the road is empty. The result is traffic chaos — equivalent to **cancer, diabetes, neurodegeneration, or immune disorders** at the cellular level.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight** — Signaling defects are broadly classified into:
> 1. **Loss-of-function (LOF)** defects — reduced/absent signaling.
> 2. **Gain-of-function (GOF)** defects — hyperactive or constitutive signaling.
> 3. **Dominant-negative (DN)** defects — mutant protein antagonizes wild-type.
> 4. **Neomorphic** defects — entirely new, abnormal signaling output.

## 1.4 The Major Disease Classes Linked to Signaling Defects

| Disease Class | Canonical Signaling Defect |
|---|---|
| Cancer (solid & hematological) | Ras/MAPK, PI3K/Akt, p53, Wnt, Notch, Hedgehog |
| Type II Diabetes & Metabolic Syndrome | Insulin receptor, IRS-1/2, PI3K/Akt, GLUT4 translocation |
| Neurodegenerative disorders | BDNF/TrkB, mTOR, autophagy (BECN1), mitochondrial apoptosis |
| Immunodeficiency | JAK/STAT (SCID), T-cell receptor (TCR) signaling |
| Cardiovascular | GPCR (β-adrenergic), calcium handling (SERCA, RyR) |
| Developmental disorders | Hedgehog (holoprosencephaly), Notch (Alagille syndrome) |

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Mutation rate in cancer drivers:** ~**1–10 mutations per Mb** in coding regions across most solid tumors.
> - **Knudson two-hit threshold** for tumor suppressors: typically **2 allelic hits** required.
> - **Standard working concentrations** in pharmacological rescue: IC50 values in the **nM to μM** range for most targeted inhibitors (e.g., Imatinib IC50 ≈ **600 nM** for BCR-ABL).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Defective Signaling Circuit

A canonical signal transduction module is composed of:
1. **Extracellular cue** (ligand, stress, nutrient, damage).
2. **Receptor/sensor** (RTK, GPCR, cytokine receptor, ion channel).
3. **Relay modules** (adaptors like GRB2, kinases like RAF, second messengers like cAMP, IP3, Ca2+).
4. **Effector machinery** (transcription factors, metabolic enzymes, cytoskeletal regulators).
5. **Negative feedback** (phosphatases, ubiquitin ligases, receptor internalization).

A defect at **any one** of these nodes is sufficient to corrupt the entire output.

## 2.2 Mechanistic Classification of Defects

### A. Loss-of-Function (LOF) Defects
- Truncating / nonsense / frameshift mutations.
- Deletions or loss of heterozygosity (LOH).
- Epigenetic silencing (promoter hypermethylation).
- **Example:** *PTEN* loss → unopposed PI3K/Akt → survival advantage.
- **Example:** *p53* LOF → impaired G1/S checkpoint.

### B. Gain-of-Function (GOF) Defects
- Activating missense mutations in oncogenes.
- Gene amplifications (e.g., *MYCN* in neuroblastoma, *ERBB2/HER2* in breast cancer).
- Chromosomal translocations producing constitutively active fusion proteins (e.g., **BCR-ABL** in CML).
- Autocrine / paracrine signaling loops (e.g., IL-6 in multiple myeloma).

### C. Dominant-Negative Defects
- Mutant protein oligomerizes with WT and inactivates it.
- **Example:** *p53* dominant-negative mutants (R175H, R248Q) form inactive tetramers.
- **Example:** *IKBα* super-repressor — blocks NF-κB activation.

### D. Neomorphic Defects
- Mutant protein acquires a novel biochemical function.
- **Example:** *IDH1/2* mutations produce **2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG)** — an oncometabolite that inhibits TET2 DNA demethylases.

## 2.3 The Hallmarks-of-Cancer Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> Hanahan & Weinberg's **Hallmarks of Cancer (2011, updated 2022)** are direct consequences of signaling defects. KTU examiners frequently test this mapping.

| Hallmark | Defective Pathway |
|---|---|
| Sustained proliferative signaling | Ras/MAPK, PI3K/Akt, Wnt/β-catenin |
| Evading growth suppressors | Rb, p53, TGF-β |
| Resisting cell death | Bcl-2 family, IAPs, p53 |
| Enabling replicative immortality | Telomerase (TERT) |
| Inducing angiogenesis | VEGF/VEGFR, HIF-1α |
| Activating invasion & metastasis | EMT (Snail, Twist), MMPs |
| Deregulating cellular energetics | AMPK, mTOR, MYC |
| Avoiding immune destruction | PD-1/PD-L1, CTLA-4 |
| Tumor-promoting inflammation | NF-κB, STAT3, IL-6 |
| Genome instability & mutation | BRCA1/2, MMR, p53 |
| Unlocking phenotypic plasticity | Notch, Wnt, YAP/TAZ |
| Senescent cells | SASP (NF-κB, mTOR) |

## 2.4 Mathematical Modeling of Defective Pathways

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme often asks to derive steady-state behavior of a defective cascade. The gold-standard model is the **Goldbeter–Koshland switch** for ultra-sensitive kinase/phosphatase systems.

### 2.4.1 Hill Function for Ligand-Receptor Binding

$$v = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot [L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n}$$

Where:
- $V_{\max}$ — maximum signaling velocity.
- $K_d$ — dissociation constant.
- $n$ — Hill coefficient (cooperativity index).

### 2.4.2 Hill Coefficient Interpretation for Defects

- **$n \approx 1$** — Michaelian, no cooperativity (healthy ligand binding).
- **$n > 1$** — Positive cooperativity (sigmoid response, robust switching).
- **$n \to \infty$** — Ultrasensitive / bistable switch (often seen in constitutive oncogenic activation).
- **$n < 1$** — Negative cooperativity (desensitization defects).

### 2.4.3 Michaelis-Menten for Receptor Downstream Kinase

$$v = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

In a **GOF receptor mutation** (e.g., EGFR L858R), $K_m$ decreases → increased flux at low ligand → ligand-independent firing.

### 2.4.4 Goldbeter–Koshland Ultrasensitivity

For a cycle where kinase K phosphorylates substrate S to SP, and phosphatase P dephosphorylates back:

$$\frac{[SP]}{[S]_{total}} = \frac{\alpha \cdot \beta - \alpha}{\beta - \alpha + 1 - \alpha \beta}$$

Where $\alpha = \frac{[K]}{K_m^K}$ and $\beta = \frac{[P]}{K_m^P}$.

- When $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are balanced, the curve becomes **steep (sigmoidal)**, producing near-digital ON/OFF behavior.
- **Cancer-relevant:** mutations that raise $\alpha$ (kinase activating) or lower $\beta$ (phosphatase loss) → cells locked in the "ON" state.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates all mathematical and conceptual relations required for this topic. Pipes have been replaced with `\mid` to preserve markdown integrity.**

| Concept | Equation / Statement | Variables & Units | Engineering/Medical Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ligand binding | $v = \frac{V_{\max} [L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n}$ | $V_{\max}$ in M/s, $K_d$ in M, $n$ dimensionless | Pharmacodynamic modeling of agonist response |
| MM kinetics | $v = \frac{V_{\max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | $K_m$ in M | GOF mutation reduces $K_m$ |
| Lineweaver-Burk | $\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_m}{V_{\max}} \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{\max}}$ | Reciprocal plot slope | Diagnostic for competitive vs non-competitive inhibitors |
| Hill slope | $n_H = \frac{d \log(v/(V_{\max}-v))}{d \log [L]}$ | Dimensionless | Predicts bistability (oncogenic switches) |
| Goldbeter-Koshland | $\frac{[SP]}{[S_T]} = \frac{\alpha\beta - \alpha}{\beta - \alpha + 1 - \alpha\beta}$ | $\alpha, \beta$ are normalized kinase/phosphatase activities | Models ultrasensitive MAPK/PI3K cascades |
| Two-hit (Knudson) | $P(\text{disease}) = 1 - (1-p)^2$ | $p$ = mutation probability per allele | Risk of LOF tumor suppressor disease |
| IC50 (Cheng-Prusoff) | $K_i = \frac{IC_{50}}{1 + [S]/K_m}$ | All in M | Predicts inhibitor potency vs WT vs mutant |
| Receptor occupancy | $f_{bound} = \frac{[L]}{K_d + [L]}$ | Dimensionless fraction | Defects shift effective $K_d$ |
| Allele frequency | $MAF = \frac{\text{mutant reads}}{\text{total reads}}$ | Dimensionless | Liquid biopsy biomarker threshold ~ **0.01–0.001** |
| Effective dose | $ED_{50}$ | mg/kg or nM | Therapeutic window definition |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Therapeutic Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "defect-to-drug" pipeline is a **direct application of systems biology**:
> 1. Identify the defective node (e.g., *BRAF V600E*).
> 2. Map its pathway (MAPK/ERK).
> 3. Design an inhibitor (Vemurafenib).
> 4. Pharmacokinetically optimize (IC50, bioavailability).
> 5. Monitor via circulating tumor DNA (allelic fraction).

This is precisely the **Drug Discovery → Clinical Translation** workflow KTU expects students to articulate in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hill Coefficient from Cooperative Binding

**Goal:** Show that the Hill coefficient quantifies ultrasensitivity, and explain how oncogenic GOF mutations alter $n$.

### Step 1 — Start with the basic binding equilibrium

For a multimeric receptor $R$ with $n$ ligand binding sites:

$$R + nL \rightleftharpoons RL_n$$

### Step 2 — Define the macroscopic dissociation constant

$$K_d = \frac{[R][L]^n}{[RL_n]}$$

### Step 3 — Derive fractional occupancy

Let $f$ be the fraction of receptors with all $n$ sites occupied:

$$f = \frac{[RL_n]}{[R]_{total}} = \frac{[RL_n]}{[R] + [RL_n]}$$

Substituting the $K_d$ expression:

$$f = \frac{[L]^n / K_d}{1 + [L]^n / K_d} = \frac{[L]^n}{K_d + [L]^n}$$

This is the standard Hill equation.

### Step 4 — Take logarithms to extract the Hill coefficient

$$Y = \log \left( \frac{f}{1-f} \right) = n \log[L] - \log K_d$$

The slope of $Y$ vs $\log[L]$ plotted on a **Hill plot** is the **Hill coefficient $n$**.

### Step 5 — Connect to oncogenic GOF mutations

- A **constitutively active mutant** receptor (e.g., EGFR L858R) is locked in a pre-activated conformation. This corresponds to shifting the equilibrium $R \rightleftharpoons R^*$ toward $R^*$ even when $[L] = 0$.
- Mathematically, the basal activation is modeled by adding a ligand-independent term:

$$f = \frac{\alpha + \beta [L]^n}{1 + \alpha + \beta [L]^n}$$

Where:
- $\beta = 1/K_d$ is the ligand-dependent activation.
- $\alpha$ is the ligand-independent (constitutive) activation.

In EGFR L858R, $\alpha \gg 0$ even when $[L] \to 0$, so $f \to \alpha / (1+\alpha)$, a non-zero basal signaling.

### Step 6 — Clinical interpretation

A high Hill coefficient + high $\alpha$ = the cell behaves like a **binary switch** stuck ON. Targeted therapies (e.g., Osimertinib for EGFR-mutant NSCLC) work by **lowering the effective $V_{\max}$** of the mutant pathway, but resistance often emerges from secondary $n$ shifts (e.g., C797S tertiary mutation).

## 3.2 Derivation: Knudson's Two-Hit Hypothesis

**Goal:** Derive the probability of biallelic LOF given per-allele mutation rate $p$.

### Step 1 — Define the per-allele probability

Each allele has an independent probability $p$ of being hit in a given cell generation.

### Step 2 — Probability that an allele is NOT hit

$$P(\text{surviving allele}) = 1 - p$$

### Step 3 — Probability that BOTH alleles remain intact

$$P(\text{0 hits}) = (1-p)(1-p) = (1-p)^2$$

### Step 4 — Probability of LOF (at least one allele hit)

$$P(\text{LOF in heterozygote}) = 1 - (1-p)^2$$

### Step 5 — Numerical example

If $p = 0.001$ per allele per generation (a typical somatic mutation rate for tumor suppressors in early life):

$$P(\text{LOF}) = 1 - (0.999)^2 = 1 - 0.998001 = 0.001999 \approx 0.2\%$$

This explains why retinoblastoma — which requires **two RB1 hits** — is rare in the general population but near-100% penetrant in **hereditary** cases (one allele already mutant, so $p = 0.001$ now hits the remaining WT allele with $P = 0.001$ per cell generation, much higher than $0.002$ for sporadic two independent hits).

## 3.3 Derivation: Goldbeter-Koshland Switch — Ultrasensitive Response in a Defective Kinase/Phosphatase Cycle

**Goal:** Show that kinase hyperactivation (a GOF defect) yields an **all-or-none** response.

### Step 1 — Write the kinase reaction

$$S + ATP \xrightarrow{K} SP + ADP \quad ; \quad v_K = \frac{V_K [S]}{K_m^K + [S]}$$

### Step 2 — Write the phosphatase reaction

$$SP + H_2O \xrightarrow{P} S + P_i \quad ; \quad v_P = \frac{V_P [SP]}{K_m^P + [SP]}$$

### Step 3 — At steady state, $v_K = v_P}$

$$\frac{V_K [S]}{K_m^K + [S]} = \frac{V_P [SP]}{K_m^P + [SP]}$$

### Step 4 — Define normalized activity parameters

$$\alpha = \frac{V_K}{K_m^K}, \quad \beta = \frac{V_P}{K_m^P}$$

Assuming $[S] + [SP] = [S]_{total}$, substituting and solving for the fraction phosphorylated:

$$\frac{[SP]}{[S]_{total}} = \frac{\alpha \beta - \alpha}{\beta - \alpha + 1 - \alpha \beta}$$

### Step 5 — Analysis of the GOF case

Suppose a tumor cell has $V_K$ doubled (e.g., BRAF V600E) and $V_P$ halved (e.g., DUSP6 downregulation):

- $\alpha$ doubles.
- $\beta$ halves.

The curve of $[SP]/[S]_{total}$ vs $\alpha$ becomes **ultra-steep** — meaning a tiny further increase in $\alpha$ pushes the cell from 10% → 90% phosphorylation in a near-discontinuous jump.

### Step 6 — Biological interpretation

This is why **bistable switches** (e.g., Cdk1-cyclin B in mitotic entry) and **oncogenic kinases** (Raf, PI3K, Src) behave as digital toggles rather than analog dimmers — a critical concept for understanding tumor cell-fate decisions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Defect → Pathway → Disease → Therapy

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **highest-yield study matrix** for KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize the mapping.

| Defective Node | Mutation Type | Pathway | Disease | Targeted Therapy | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| *EGFR* L858R / T790M / C797S | GOF (exon 19 del) | RTK / Ras / MAPK | NSCLC (lung adenocarcinoma) | Gefitinib, Erlotinib, Osimertinib | Reversible/irreversible TK inhibitor |
| *BRAF* V600E | GOF | MAPK | Melanoma, hairy cell leukemia, thyroid | Vemurafenib, Dabrafenib | ATP-competitive BRAF inhibitor |
| *KRAS* G12D / G12V | GOF (locked-GTP) | MAPK / PI3K | Pancreatic, colorectal, lung | Sotorasib (G12C), MRTX1133 (G12D) | Covalent KRAS-G12C/D inhibitor |
| *BCR-ABL* (t(9;22)) | GOF fusion | Constitutive tyrosine kinase | CML, Ph+ ALL | Imatinib, Dasatinib | BCR-ABL ATP-pocket inhibitor |
| *HER2/ERBB2* amplification | GOF | PI3K / MAPK | Breast, gastric | Trastuzumab, Lapatinib | mAb / TKI |
| *p53* R175H, R248Q | LOF + DN | DNA damage response, apoptosis | Li-Fraumeni, >50% cancers | APR-246 (eprenetapopt) | Refolds mutant p53 |
| *RB1* LOF | LOF | G1/S checkpoint | Retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma | None specific; CDK4/6 inhibitors upstream | Palbociclib |
| *PTEN* LOF | LOF | PI3K/Akt | Glioblastoma, Cowden syndrome | PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib) | Indirect rescue |
| *JAK2* V617F | GOF | JAK/STAT | Polycythemia vera, MPN | Ruxolitinib | JAK1/2 inhibitor |
| *IDH1* R132H | Neomorphic | DNA methylation, 2-HG | Glioma, AML | Ivosidenib | Inhibits mutant IDH1 |
| *NF1* LOF | LOF (Ras-GAP) | Ras/MAPK | Neurofibromatosis type 1 | MEK inhibitors (Selumetinib) | Downstream blockade |
| *BRCA1/2* LOF | LOF | Homologous recombination | Breast, ovarian | Olaparib, Talazoparib | PARP inhibition (synthetic lethality) |
| *PIK3CA* H1047R | GOF | PI3K/Akt | Breast, colorectal | Alpelisib | PI3Kα-selective inhibitor |
| *APC* truncation | LOF | Wnt/β-catenin | Familial adenomatous polyposis, CRC | Tankyrase inhibitors (investigational) | Restores β-catenin degradation |
| *NOTCH1* LOF / GOF | Both context-dependent | Notch | T-ALL, Alagille, CSCC | γ-secretase inhibitors, DLL4 mAbs | Context-dependent |
| *ALK* fusion (EML4-ALK) | GOF fusion | MAPK / PI3K / STAT3 | NSCLC (young, non-smokers) | Crizotinib, Alectinib | ALK TKI |
| *MYC* amplification | GOF | Transcription | Burkitt lymphoma, neuroblastoma | BET inhibitors (investigational) | Indirect via BRD4 |
| *Wnt* pathway | GOF (ligand) | Wnt/β-catenin | Colorectal, hepatocellular | Porcupine inhibitors (LGK974) | Blocks Wnt secretion |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — IC50 of an Inhibitor Against a GOF Mutant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** A novel kinase inhibitor has an IC50 of **250 nM** against the wild-type receptor and **18 nM** against the L858R mutant. Calculate the therapeutic selectivity index. The substrate concentration in the assay is $5 \mu M$ and the $K_m$ for ATP is **40 μM**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Cheng-Prusoff equation**

$$K_i = \frac{IC_{50}}{1 + [S]/K_m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_i$ for WT**

$$K_i^{WT} = \frac{250 \, \text{nM}}{1 + (5000/40)} = \frac{250}{1 + 125} = \frac{250}{126} = 1.984 \, \text{nM}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_i$ for L858R**

$$K_i^{L858R} = \frac{18 \, \text{nM}}{126} = 0.143 \, \text{nM}$$

**Step 4 — Therapeutic selectivity index (SI)**

$$SI = \frac{K_i^{WT}}{K_i^{L858R}} = \frac{1.984}{0.143} \approx 13.9$$

**Step 5 — Clinical interpretation**

A selectivity index >10 is considered **favorable** — the drug preferentially inhibits the mutant without significantly affecting WT receptors in normal tissues. This is the engineering rationale for targeted cancer therapy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Classification of Signaling Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Signaling Defects] --> B[Loss of Function LOF]
    A --> C[Gain of Function GOF]
    A --> D[Dominant Negative DN]
    A --> E[Neomorphic]

    B --> B1[Truncating mutations]
    B --> B2[Promoter hypermethylation]
    B --> B3[Deletions and LOH]
    B --> B4[PTEN LOF]
    B --> B5[p53 LOF]
    B --> B6[BRCA1 LOF]

    C --> C1[Activating missense]
    C --> C2[Gene amplification]
    C --> C3[Chromosomal translocation]
    C --> C4[Autocrine loop]
    C --> C5[EGFR L858R]
    C --> C6[BCR ABL]
    C --> C7[KRAS G12D]

    D --> D1[p53 R175H]
    D --> D2[IKB super repressor]

    E --> E1[IDH1 R132H produces 2 HG]
    E --> E2[BCR Abl novel substrates]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Defect-to-Drug Pipeline (Translational Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Identify defective node] --> P2[Genomic profiling NGS]
    P2 --> P3[Map pathway]
    P3 --> P4[In silico docking]
    P4 --> P5[Medicinal chemistry optimization]
    P5 --> P6[In vitro IC50 assay]
    P6 --> P7[Pharmacokinetics]
    P7 --> P8[Phase I II III trials]
    P8 --> P9[Clinical use]
    P9 --> P10[Monitor ctDNA allele fraction]
    P10 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Bistable Switch in Oncogenic Kinase Signaling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Inactive signaling] -->|Kinase activation GOF mutation| A
    A[Active MAPK PI3K cascade]
    A -->|Positive feedback| A
    A -->|Ultrasensitive response| B[Phosphorylated SP high]
    A -->|Loss of phosphatase feedback| C[Locked ON state]
    C --> D[Proliferation, survival, invasion]
    B --> D
    D -->|Targeted TKI| E[Apoptosis or senescence]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Cross-Talk Between Defective Pathways in Cancer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Receptor RTK GPCR] -->|GOF mutation| M1[Ras MAPK]
    R -->|GOF mutation| M2[PI3K Akt]
    M1 --> TF1[ERK]
    M2 --> TF2[Akt]
    TF1 --> CC[Cell cycle Cyclin D]
    TF2 --> CC
    TF2 --> AP[Anti apoptosis Bcl 2]
    TF1 --> AP
    M1 -.cross talk.-> M2
    M2 -.cross talk.-> M1
    P53[TP53 LOF] -.removes checkpoint.-> CC
    PTEN[PTEN LOF] -.amplifies.-> M2
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Hallmarks of Cancer from Defective Signaling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Sustained proliferation] -->|Ras, PI3K, Wnt| CC[Oncogenic output]
    H2[Evading growth suppression] -->|Rb, p53, TGFb| CC
    H3[Resisting cell death] -->|Bcl2, IAP, p53| CC
    H4[Replicative immortality] -->|TERT| CC
    H5[Angiogenesis] -->|VEGF, HIF1a| CC
    H6[Invasion and metastasis] -->|EMT, MMP| CC
    H7[Deregulated energetics] -->|AMPK, mTOR, MYC| CC
    H8[Immune evasion] -->|PD1, PDL1, CTLA4| CC
    H9[Tumor inflammation] -->|NFkB, STAT3| CC
    H10[Genome instability] -->|BRCA, MMR, p53| CC
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between gain-of-function and loss-of-function signaling defects with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Gain-of-Function (GOF) | Loss-of-Function (LOF) |
|---|---|---|
| Effect on pathway output | Hyperactive or constitutive | Reduced or absent |
| Nature of mutation | Activating missense, amplification, translocation | Truncating, nonsense, deletion, silencing |
| Cancer association | Oncogene (e.g., KRAS, BRAF) | Tumor suppressor (e.g., p53, PTEN) |
| Inheritance | Dominant (one hit sufficient) | Recessive (two-hit, Knudson) |
| Example | EGFR L858R in NSCLC | p53 mutation in Li-Fraumeni |
| Therapy | Targeted inhibitors (TKIs) | Indirect (synthetic lethality, restoration) |

*(Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example of each, 1 mark for table/comparison.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Explain the concept of a dominant-negative mutation in p53 with its clinical relevance."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A dominant-negative (DN) mutation produces a mutant p53 protein that oligomerizes with wild-type (WT) p53, forming inactive tetramers. **(1 mark)**
- Common hotspot DN mutants include R175H (DNA-binding domain) and R248Q, R273H. These impair WT p53's ability to bind DNA and activate target genes like *p21*, *BAX*, *PUMA*. **(1 mark)**
- **Clinical relevance:** ~50% of all solid tumors carry *TP53* mutations, most of which are DN. Li-Fraumeni syndrome patients inherit one DN mutant allele and develop diverse early-onset cancers. The DN nature is why *TP53* behaves as an oncogene-driver despite being a tumor suppressor. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Signaling Defects in Cancer (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

#### Part (a) — Discuss the molecular basis of oncogenic Ras signaling defects. Highlight the role of KRAS G12D in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Introduction to Ras (1 mark)**
   - The Ras family (HRAS, NRAS, KRAS4A, KRAS4B) are small GTPases that act as molecular switches in RTK-driven MAPK and PI3K signaling.
   - They cycle between inactive GDP-bound and active GTP-bound states, regulated by GEFs (e.g., SOS) and GAPs (e.g., NF1, p120GAP).

2. **KRAS G12D molecular mechanism (3 marks)**
   - Glycine at codon 12 is critical for proper positioning of the catalytic loop. Substitution by aspartate (G12D) creates a steric clash that prevents GAP-mediated hydrolysis of GTP.
   - The protein is therefore locked in the **active GTP-bound state** → constitutive MAPK (RAF-MEK-ERK) and PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling.
   - Effectors: RAF (MAPK), PI3K (AKT), RalGDS, TIAM1 (Rac).

3. **Clinical / pancreatic cancer context (2 marks)**
   - KRAS G12D is the most common mutation in **pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC)**, present in **>90%** of cases.
   - Cooperating mutations: *TP53*, *SMAD4*, *CDKN2A* — defining the four-hit PDAC signature.
   - Therapeutic challenge: KRAS was long considered "undruggable" due to its smooth surface and picomolar GTP affinity. **Sotorasib** (AMG 510) and **MRTX1133** have now succeeded via covalent binding to the G12C/G12D mutant cysteine pocket.

4. **Targeted strategies and resistance (1 mark)**
   - Direct KRAS inhibitors, SHP2 inhibitors (upstream), MEK inhibitors (downstream), immune checkpoint blockade (KRAS-G12D produces neoantigens recognized by T cells in PDAC immunotherapy trials).

#### Part (b) — Describe the defect in the PI3K/Akt pathway due to PTEN loss. How is this therapeutically exploited? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **PTEN structure and function (1.5 marks)**
   - PTEN (Phosphatase and Tensin homolog) is a **dual-specificity phosphatase** that dephosphorylates **PIP3 → PIP2**, antagonizing PI3K.
   - It acts as a brake on PI3K/Akt signaling.

2. **Consequence of PTEN loss (2.5 marks)**
   - PIP3 accumulates → **Akt** is recruited to the membrane and phosphorylated by PDK1 and mTORC2.
   - Activated Akt phosphorylates >100 substrates: GSK3β (cell cycle), BAD (anti-apoptotic), FOXO (cytoplasmic retention), TSC2 (mTOR activation), MDM2 (p53 degradation).
   - Phenotype: enhanced proliferation, survival, metabolic reprogramming (Warburg effect), invasion.
   - Cowden syndrome (germline PTEN LOF) and many sporadic cancers (glioblastoma, prostate, endometrial) show this defect.

3. **Therapeutic exploitation (2 marks)**
   - **PI3Kα inhibitors:** Alpelisib for PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer.
   - **AKT inhibitors:** Capivasertib (AZD5363) — approved for HR+ breast cancer with PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN alterations.
   - **mTOR inhibitors:** Everolimus, Temsirolimus.
   - **Synthetic lethality:** PARP inhibitors in PTEN-deficient tumors (impaired HR repair).
   - **Immune contexture:** PTEN loss correlates with immunosuppressive TME; combination with PD-1 blockade under investigation.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark)**
   - The PI3K-Akt pathway is among the most frequently dysregulated in human cancers. PTEN loss is the prototype LOF tumor suppressor defect, and modern oncology translates this knowledge into precision therapy.

---

### Question B: Alternative Module 4 Long Answer (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the JAK-STAT pathway and how JAK2 V617F mutation leads to myeloproliferative neoplasms. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **JAK-STAT pathway (2 marks)**
   - Cytokine receptors (e.g., EPO-R, TPO-R, G-CSF-R) lack intrinsic kinase activity and rely on JAKs (Janus kinases: JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, TYK2) for signal initiation.
   - Ligand binding → receptor dimerization → JAK trans-phosphorylation → STAT recruitment → STAT phosphorylation → STAT dimerization → nuclear translocation → gene transcription.

2. **JAK2 V617F molecular defect (2.5 marks)**
   - The V617F mutation occurs in the **JH2 pseudokinase (auto-inhibitory) domain**.
   - Valine → Phenylalanine disrupts the inhibitory JH1-JH2 interaction.
   - Result: **constitutive JAK2 activation** even in the absence of erythropoietin or thrombopoietin.
   - Downstream: hyperactive STAT5 → enhanced erythroid/myeloid proliferation.

3. **Clinical phenotype (1.5 marks)**
   - **Polycythemia vera (PV)**, **essential thrombocythemia (ET)**, and **primary myelofibrosis (PMF)** all carry JAK2 V617F in ~50–95% of cases.
   - Phenotype: erythrocytosis, thrombocytosis, leukocytosis, splenomegaly, and risk of progression to AML.

4. **Therapeutic strategy (1 mark)**
   - **Ruxolitinib** (JAK1/2 inhibitor) is first-line for symptomatic PMF and steroid-refractory GVHD. It reduces constitutional symptoms and splenomegaly but does not eliminate the malignant clone.

#### Part (b) — Write a note on synthetic lethality in BRCA-mutated tumors. Mention the role of PARP inhibitors. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Concept of synthetic lethality (2 marks)**
   - Two genes are synthetically lethal if mutation in either is tolerated, but simultaneous loss kills the cell.
   - This is exploited when one gene is already defective in tumor cells; targeting the second gene selectively kills tumor but spares normal cells.

2. **BRCA1/2 biology (1.5 marks)**
   - BRCA1 and BRCA2 are essential for **homologous recombination (HR)**, a high-fidelity DNA double-strand break repair pathway active in S/G2.
   - Germline BRCA1/2 mutations predispose to breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and prostate cancers.
   - Tumor cells with BRCA LOF become HR-deficient (HRD) and rely on alternative pathways — chiefly **base excision repair (BER)** via **PARP1**.

3. **PARP inhibition mechanism (2.5 marks)**
   - **PARP inhibitors** (Olaparib, Talazoparib, Rucaparib, Niraparib) trap PARP on DNA, producing replication-fork stalling.
   - Stalled forks in BRCA-deficient cells cannot be rescued by HR → collapse into double-strand breaks → cell death.
   - In HR-proficient normal cells, the breaks are accurately repaired — hence the **wide therapeutic window**.

4. **Clinical evidence and resistance (1 mark)**
   - Olaparib is FDA-approved for BRCA-mutant ovarian, breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers.
   - Resistance emerges via secondary BRCA reversion mutations, loss of 53BP1 (shifts repair back to NHEJ), or upregulation of drug efflux pumps (ABCB1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Signaling Defect Questions (KTU Pattern)**
> 1. **Confusing oncogenes with tumor suppressors** — students often classify p53 as an oncogene because its mutation drives cancer. Always clarify: p53 is a **tumor suppressor**; its mutation is LOF and usually DN, NOT a GOF oncogenic event.
> 2. **Writing "Ras is a kinase"** — Ras is a **GTPase**, not a kinase. RAF is the kinase in the cascade. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.
> 3. **Omitting the two-hit concept** for tumor suppressors — Knudson's hypothesis is mandatory.
> 4. **Skipping the example drug** when discussing a defect — KTU examiners reward "defect → drug" linkage with 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing "BRCA causes cancer"** — incorrect. BRCA LOF **predisposes** to cancer; cancer arises only when the second allele is also lost.
> 6. **Failing to specify which pathway** (MAPK vs PI3K vs JAK-STAT) the defect perturbs — a 14-mark answer without pathway-specificity loses 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Ignoring drug resistance mechanisms** in the therapeutic part — a common "must-mention" for full marks.
> 8. **Mixing up EGFR and HER2** — EGFR = HER1; HER2 = ERBB2/NEU. Trastuzumab targets HER2, not EGFR.
> 9. **Forgetting units and magnitudes** in calculations (e.g., $K_d$ in nM, IC50 in nM) — KTU specifically checks numerical literacy.
> 10. **Writing generic answers about "cancer"** without specifying tumor type — always cite the canonical tumor (e.g., EGFR → lung, BRAF → melanoma, BCR-ABL → CML).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Signaling Defects (Module 4)**

### A. Core Definitions
- **Signaling defect** = any alteration in a signal transduction component leading to aberrant cellular output.
- **LOF (loss-of-function)** = reduced/absent signaling (tumor suppressor model, Knudson two-hit).
- **GOF (gain-of-function)** = hyperactive/constitutive signaling (oncogene model, dominant, one-hit).
- **Dominant-negative (DN)** = mutant antagonizes WT within the same multimeric complex.
- **Neomorphic** = entirely new function (e.g., IDH1 R132H → 2-HG).

### B. Must-Know Molecular Defects
- **RTK:** EGFR L858R, T790M, C797S; HER2 amplification; FGFR3 S249C.
- **Ras/MAPK:** KRAS G12D/V, BRAF V600E, NF1 LOF, MEK1 mutations.
- **PI3K/Akt:** PIK3CA H1047R, PTEN LOF, AKT1 E17K, mTOR activating.
- **JAK-STAT:** JAK2 V617F, STAT3/5 GOF, SOCS silencing.
- **Tumor suppressors:** p53, RB1, APC, BRCA1/2, VHL, NF1, NF2.
- **Transcription/Myc:** MYC translocation, MYCN amplification.
- **Death pathway:** Bcl-2 overexpression, BAX LOF, caspase-8 silencing.
- **Metabolic:** IDH1/2 R132H/R172K (neomorphic), SDH/FH LOF (HIF stabilization).
- **Developmental:** Notch1, Hedgehog (SMO M2, PTCH1 LOF), Wnt (APC, β-catenin).

### C. Mathematical / Quantitative Essentials
- Hill equation: $f = \frac{[L]^n}{K_d^n + [L]^n}$
- Michaelis-Menten: $v = \frac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$
- Cheng-Prusoff: $K_i = \frac{IC_{50}}{1 + [S]/K_m}$
- Goldbeter-Koshland: ultrasensitivity in kinase/phosphatase cycles.
- Knudson: $P(\text{LOF}) = 1 - (1-p)^2$.

### D. Therapeutic Modalities to Pair
- **Small-molecule TKI** for RTK/intracellular kinases.
- **Monoclonal antibody** for extracellular receptors (trastuzumab, cetuximab).
- **Synthetic lethality** (PARP-BRCA).
- **Gene therapy** (p53 restoration: Gendicine, China-approved).
- **Immunotherapy** (PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors effective in MSI-high, TMB-high tumors).
- **PROTACs** (newer modality degrading oncoproteins).

### E. Cross-Cutting Concepts
- **Bistability** → digital switching → robust oncogenic states.
- **Feedback** → negative (loss = hyperactivation), positive (gain = runaway signaling).
- **Crosstalk** → MAPK ↔ PI3K; Notch ↔ Wnt; JAK-STAT ↔ PI3K.
- **Tumor heterogeneity** → clonal evolution under selective pressure of therapy.
- **Germline vs somatic** → hereditary cancer syndromes (BRCA, Li-Fraumeni, FAP, MEN2).

### F. The "Golden Chain" for Any Signaling Defect Question
> **Receptor (defect) → Adapter/Scaffold → Kinase cascade (defect) → Transcription factor (defect) → Cellular output → Disease → Therapy**

Master this chain for any pathway, and you can answer virtually any KTU 14-marker.

### G. High-Yield Mnemonics
- **"STOP GOF"** — **S**rc, **T**yrosine kinases, **O**ncogenes → **P**hosphorylation dominant.
- **"RB PTEN p53"** — **R**etino**B**lastoma, **P**hosphatase, **P**53 → tumor suppressor trifecta.
- **"Two-hit Knudson"** — Tumor suppressors need 2 hits; oncogenes need 1.

### H. Key Clinical Numbers to Memorize
- KRAS G12D prevalence in PDAC: **>90%**
- BRAF V600E in melanoma: **~50%**
- p53 mutation frequency in solid tumors: **~50%**
- HER2 amplification in breast cancer: **~15–20%**
- BRCA1/2 lifetime breast cancer risk: **~70%**
- JAK2 V617F in polycythemia vera: **>95%**
- Knudson 2-hit: **two independent mutational events**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of physiological roles (apoptosis, cell cycle regulation, gene transcription) and clinical significance (cancer, cardiovascular disease, learning and memory, immune responses)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT742 - CELL SIGNALING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples of physiological roles (apoptosis, cell cycle regulation, gene transcription) and clinical significance (cancer, cardiovascular disease, learning and memory, immune responses)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Physiological Role of Cell Signaling"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> Cell signaling is not an isolated event — every signaling pathway executes a **physiological function** in the body (apoptosis, division, gene expression) and any defect in it translates into a **clinical pathology** (cancer, heart disease, neurodegeneration, immunodeficiency).

Cell signaling pathways are the *molecular communication network* of the body. After a ligand binds its receptor, the signal is propagated through second messengers, kinases, and transcription factors, ultimately changing cell behavior. These behavioral outputs fall into three broad physiological categories:

| Physiological Role | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Apoptosis** | Programmed cell death — the cell "commits suicide" when instructed. |
| **Cell Cycle Regulation** | Tight control of when a cell divides, ensuring genomic integrity. |
| **Gene Transcription** | Conversion of extracellular cues into altered gene expression profiles. |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "City Control Room"

Imagine a city as a multicellular organism:

- **Hormones/ligands** = phone calls from the mayor's office.
- **Receptors** = switchboard operators at each department.
- **Second messengers & kinases** = messengers running between floors carrying memos.
- **Transcription factors** = city planners who rewrite the city's blueprint (DNA) based on the message.
- **Physiological output** = the actual city action — closing a road (apoptosis), opening a new flyover (cell division), or changing zoning laws (gene transcription).

When any link in this chain breaks, the "city" develops diseases — traffic jams (cancer), power grid failure (cardiovascular disease), memory loss (neurodegeneration), or a riot (autoimmune disease).

---

## 1.2 Apoptosis — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Apoptosis** is an energy-dependent, genetically encoded process of programmed cell death characterized by cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, membrane blebbing, and formation of **apoptotic bodies** that are phagocytosed without inflammation.

Apoptosis is mediated by a family of cysteine proteases called **caspases** that exist as inactive **procaspases (zymogens)** and are activated by proteolytic cleavage.

## 1.3 Cell Cycle Regulation — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cell cycle regulation** refers to the tightly orchestrated sequence of events (G1 → S → G2 → M) controlled by cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs), cyclins, and checkpoint proteins that ensure faithful DNA replication and chromosome segregation.

## 1.4 Gene Transcription — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Signal-dependent gene transcription** is the process by which extracellular signals modulate the activity of transcription factors (e.g., NF-κB, STAT, CREB), enabling cells to reprogram gene expression in response to environmental cues.

---

## 1.5 Clinical Significance Overview

| Disease | Signaling Link |
|---|---|
| **Cancer** | Loss of apoptosis + uncontrolled proliferation + unchecked transcription (oncogenic pathways). |
| **Cardiovascular Disease** | Dysregulated β-adrenergic, calcium, and GPCR signaling. |
| **Learning and Memory** | NMDA receptor / Ca²⁺ / CREB signaling underlies Long-Term Potentiation (LTP). |
| **Immune Responses** | T-cell receptor (TCR), B-cell receptor (BCR), JAK-STAT, and NF-κB signaling. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Apoptosis — Two Major Pathways

### A. Intrinsic (Mitochondrial) Pathway

Triggered by **internal stress** such as DNA damage, oxidative stress, or growth factor withdrawal.

$$\text{Stress} \;\rightarrow\; \text{p53 activation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Bax/Bak oligomerization on mitochondria}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{Cytochrome c release into cytosol}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{Apoptosome formation (Apaf-1 + Cyt-c + dATP + procaspase-9)}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{Caspase-9 activation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Caspase-3/7 execution}$$

### B. Extrinsic (Death Receptor) Pathway

Triggered by **external ligands** such as FasL, TNF-α, or TRAIL binding to death receptors (FasR, TNFR1).

$$\text{Ligand-Receptor binding} \;\rightarrow\; \text{FADD recruitment} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procaspase-8 cleavage}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{Caspase-8 activation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Caspase-3/7 execution}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caspase-8 can cleave Bid → tBid**, bridging extrinsic and intrinsic pathways — this is called **Type II signaling**.

### C. Execution Phase (Common to Both)

$$\text{Caspase-3/7} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cleavage of PARP, ICAD, gelsolin, lamins}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{DNA fragmentation, membrane blebbing, apoptotic body formation}$$

---

## 2.2 Cell Cycle Regulation — Core Logic

The cell cycle is driven by **Cyclin-CDK complexes** whose activity oscillates through synthesis and ubiquitin-mediated degradation.

| Phase Transition | Cyclin-CDK Complex | Key Substrate |
|---|---|---|
| **G1 → S** | Cyclin D – CDK4/6 | **Rb protein** (phosphorylation releases E2F) |
| **G1 → S (late)** | Cyclin E – CDK2 | Rb, p27, DNA replication proteins |
| **S → G2** | Cyclin A – CDK2 | DNA replication factors |
| **G2 → M** | Cyclin B – CDK1 | Nuclear lamins, Golgi, condensins |

### Checkpoint Proteins

- **G1/S checkpoint** → governed by **Rb-E2F** axis and **p53 → p21**.
- **G2/M checkpoint** → governed by **ATR/Chk1** and **ATM/Chk2**.
- **Spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC)** → governed by **Mad2, BubR1** ensuring proper kinetochore-microtubule attachment.

$$\text{DNA damage} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ATM/ATR} \;\rightarrow\; \text{p53 stabilization} \;\rightarrow\; \text{p21 transcription} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CDK2 inhibition} \;\rightarrow\; \text{G1 arrest}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Loss of p53 (the "guardian of the genome") is the most common mutation in human cancers (~50% of all tumors).

---

## 2.3 Signal-Dependent Gene Transcription

### Major Signal-to-Nucleus Pathways

| Pathway | Stimulus | Key Transcription Factor |
|---|---|---|
| **MAPK/ERK** | Growth factors (EGF, PDGF) | Elk-1, c-Fos, c-Myc |
| **JAK-STAT** | Cytokines (IFN, IL-6) | STAT1, STAT3, STAT5 |
| **NF-κB** | TNF-α, IL-1β, LPS | NF-κB (p50/p65) |
| **cAMP/PKA/CREB** | Gs-coupled receptors | CREB (cAMP Response Element Binding protein) |
| **Wnt/β-catenin** | Wnt ligands | TCF/LEF |
| **TGF-β/Smad** | TGF-β | Smad2/3-Smad4 complex |
| **Steroid Receptors** | Hormones (estrogen, cortisol) | Direct nuclear receptors (ER, GR) |

### Canonical NF-κB Activation

$$\text{TNF-α} \;\rightarrow\; \text{TNFR} \;\rightarrow\; \text{IKK complex activation}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{IκBα phosphorylation (Ser32/36)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Ubiquitination} \;\rightarrow\; \text{26S proteasomal degradation}$$

$$\rightarrow\; \text{NF-κB (p50/p65) nuclear translocation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Transcription of anti-apoptotic, inflammatory genes}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Molecules | Direction / Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Anti-apoptotic** | Bcl-2, Bcl-xL, Mcl-1, IAP, survivin | Inhibit apoptosis |
| **Pro-apoptotic** | Bax, Bak, Bad, Bid, Bim, caspase-3/8/9 | Promote apoptosis |
| **CDK inhibitors (CKIs)** | p21 (CIP1), p27 (KIP1), p16 (INK4a) | Inhibit cell cycle |
| **Tumor suppressors** | p53, Rb, PTEN, APC | Brake on proliferation |
| **Oncogenes** | Ras, Myc, Cyclin D1, EGFR, Bcr-Abl | Accelerate proliferation |
| **Memory/LTP molecules** | NMDA-R, CaMKII, CREB, BDNF | Synaptic plasticity |
| **Immune receptors** | TCR, BCR, TLR, cytokine receptors | Trigger JAK-STAT, NF-κB |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering/Biotech Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU tests this:** Most modern drugs (Trastuzumab, Imatinib, Venetoclax, Tacrolimus) and gene therapies target these exact pathways. Understanding them is essential for **drug discovery, clinical diagnostics, and biopharmaceutical R&D**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Intrinsic Apoptosis Pathway

**Step 1 — Cellular Stress Detection**
DNA damage (double-strand breaks) is sensed by the kinase **ATM** (Ataxia-Telangiectasia Mutated).

$$\text{DSB} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ATM autophosphorylation (Ser1981)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ATM active}$$

**Step 2 — p53 Stabilization**
ATM phosphorylates p53 at **Ser15**, disrupting its interaction with MDM2 (its E3 ubiquitin ligase).

$$[\text{p53}]_{\text{steady state}} \;\propto\; \frac{k_{\text{syn}}}{k_{\text{deg}} \cdot [\text{MDM2}]}$$

When MDM2-mediated degradation is blocked, p53 accumulates in the nucleus.

**Step 3 — Transcriptional Upregulation of Pro-apoptotic Genes**
p53 binds to response elements in promoters of:
- **Bax** (mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization)
- **PUMA** and **NOXA** (Bcl-2 antagonists)
- **FasR** (death receptor — extrinsic pathway cross-talk)

**Step 4 — Mitochondrial Outer Membrane Permeabilization (MOMP)**
Bax translocates to mitochondria, oligomerizes with Bak, forming pores.

$$\text{MOMP} \;\rightarrow\; \Delta\Psi_m \text{ collapse} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cytochrome c, Smac/DIABLO release}$$

**Step 5 — Apoptosome Assembly**
In the cytosol, cytochrome c binds **Apaf-1**, inducing a conformational change that recruits **procaspase-9**.

$$\text{Cyto c + Apaf-1 + dATP} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Apoptosome (heptameric wheel)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Caspase-9 activation}$$

**Step 6 — Executioner Caspase Cascade**
Caspase-9 cleaves and activates **caspase-3** and **caspase-7**.

$$\text{Caspase-3} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cleaves PARP, ICAD, cytokeratins, fodrin} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Morphological apoptosis}$$

**Step 7 — DNA Fragmentation**
Caspase-Activated DNase (CAD) is released when its inhibitor ICAD is cleaved.

$$\text{ICAD cleavage} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CAD active} \;\rightarrow\; \text{DNA cleaved into ~180 bp oligonucleosomal fragments}$$

**End Result:** Apoptotic bodies phagocytosed by macrophages — **no inflammation**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough — G1/S Checkpoint Logic

**Step 1 — Mitogenic Signal Reception**
Growth factor (e.g., EGF) binds receptor tyrosine kinase → Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK.

**Step 2 — Cyclin D Synthesis**
ERK phosphorylates and activates transcription factors (e.g., Myc, AP-1) that drive **CCND1** transcription.

$$\text{[Cyclin D]} \uparrow \;\rightarrow\; \text{Binds CDK4/6} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cyclin D-CDK4/6 active}$$

**Step 3 — Rb Phosphorylation (The Molecular Switch)**
In quiescent (G0) cells, **Rb** is hypophosphorylated and binds **E2F**, blocking transcription.

$$\text{Hypo-Rb + E2F} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Repression complex} \;\rightarrow\; \text{S-phase genes OFF}$$

When Cyclin D-CDK4/6 phosphorylates Rb at multiple sites:

$$\text{Rb-PP} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Releases E2F} \;\rightarrow\; \text{E2F transcribes Cyclin E, DNA polymerase, thymidine kinase} \;\rightarrow\; \text{S-phase entry}$$

**Step 4 — Positive Feedback**
Cyclin E-CDK2 further phosphorylates Rb → irreversible commitment to S-phase.

**Step 5 — Brake Application**
If DNA is damaged:
$$\text{ATM} \;\rightarrow\; \text{p53} \;\rightarrow\; \text{p21} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Inhibits Cyclin E-CDK2 and Cyclin A-CDK2} \;\rightarrow\; \text{G1 arrest}$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — CREB-Mediated Transcription in Learning

**Step 1 — Glutamate Release**
Pre-synaptic neuron fires → glutamate in synaptic cleft.

**Step 2 — NMDA Receptor Activation**
NMDA-R opens **only** when both glutamate binds AND the post-synaptic membrane is depolarized (Mg²⁺ block relieved).

$$\text{Vm depolarization} + \text{Glutamate} \;\rightarrow\; \text{NMDA-R opens} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ influx}$$

**Step 3 — CaMKII Activation**
Ca²⁺ binds calmodulin → activates **CaMKII** (a memory molecule).

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{CaM} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CaM-Ca}^{2+} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CaMKII autophosphorylation (Thr286)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Ca}^{2+}/\text{CaM-independent activity}$$

**Step 4 — CREB Phosphorylation**
Several kinases (CaMKIV, PKA, MAPK) converge to phosphorylate **CREB at Ser133**.

$$\text{CREB-Ser133-P} + \text{CBP (co-activator)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Binds CRE element (TGACGTCA)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Transcription of } BDNF, c-fos, Egr1$$

**Step 5 — Late-Phase LTP**
BDNF strengthens synaptic connectivity → **long-term memory consolidation**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **CREB knock-out mice** cannot form long-term memory, even though they can still learn initially.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Disease-to-Pathway Mapping

| Disease | Defective Signaling | Molecular Lesion | Therapeutic Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cancer (many types)** | Ras-MAPK, PI3K-Akt, Rb, p53 | KRAS, BRAF, TP53, RB1 mutations | Trastuzumab (HER2), Imatinib (Bcr-Abl), Venetoclax (Bcl-2) |
| **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia** | Bcr-Abl fusion tyrosine kinase | t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome | Imatinib |
| **Cardiovascular (Heart Failure)** | β-adrenergic / Gs-AC-cAMP | β1-receptor desensitization, ↓ cAMP | β-blockers, digoxin |
| **Hypertension** | Ca²⁺ signaling in vascular smooth muscle | L-type Ca²⁺ channel overactivity | Calcium channel blockers (Amlodipine) |
| **Alzheimer's Disease** | NMDA-R overactivation, ↓ CREB | Amyloid-β excitotoxicity | Memantine (NMDA-R antagonist) |
| **SCID (immunodeficiency)** | IL-12 / IFN-γ / JAK-STAT | JAK3, IL2RG (γc) mutations | Gene therapy, JAK inhibitors |
| **Asthma / Autoimmunity** | NF-κB, JAK-STAT | Chronic inflammation | Corticosteroids, Tofacitinib |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic pathways of apoptosis. Mention one initiator caspase for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Intrinsic Pathway | Extrinsic Pathway |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Internal stress (DNA damage, growth factor withdrawal) | External ligand binding (FasL, TNF-α, TRAIL) |
| **Initiator caspase** | **Caspase-9** | **Caspase-8** |
| **Key regulator** | Bcl-2 family (Bax/Bak vs Bcl-2/Bcl-xL) | Death receptors (FasR, TNFR1) |
| **Mitochondrial involvement** | **Yes** (MOMP, cytochrome c release) | No (but cross-talk via Bid cleavage) |
| **Adaptor protein** | Apaf-1 | FADD |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> **What is the role of the Rb-E2F axis in G1/S transition of the cell cycle?**

**Model Answer:**
In quiescent cells, **hypophosphorylated Rb binds E2F** transcription factors, sequestering them and repressing transcription of S-phase genes. When **Cyclin D-CDK4/6** (and later Cyclin E-CDK2) phosphorylate Rb, E2F is released, drives transcription of Cyclin E, DNA polymerase, and thymidine kinase, and commits the cell to **S-phase**. Loss of functional Rb (as in **retinoblastoma**) leads to uncontrolled E2F activity and is a hallmark of cancer.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **mitochondrial (intrinsic) pathway of apoptosis**, highlighting the roles of Bcl-2 family proteins, cytochrome c, the apoptosome, and executioner caspases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how **defects in apoptotic signaling contribute to cancer**, with examples of specific genetic lesions and therapeutic strategies.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Triggers of the Intrinsic Pathway** (1 mark)
DNA damage (double-strand breaks), oxidative stress, growth factor deprivation, ER stress, and chemotherapeutic agents.

**Step 2 — Role of Bcl-2 Family** (2 marks)
The Bcl-2 family contains:
- **Pro-apoptotic effectors:** Bax, Bak (multidomain) — form pores in mitochondrial outer membrane.
- **BH3-only activators:** Bim, Bid, Puma, Noxa — activate Bax/Bak.
- **Anti-apoptotic:** Bcl-2, Bcl-xL, Mcl-1 — sequester Bax/Bak.

$$\text{Stress} \;\rightarrow\; \text{BH3-only} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Bax/Bak activation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{MOMP}$$

**Step 3 — MOMP and Cytochrome c Release** (1 mark)
Cytochrome c (normally in intermembrane space) enters cytosol; Smac/DIABLO also released to neutralize IAPs.

**Step 4 — Apoptosome Formation** (1 mark)
Cyt-c + Apaf-1 + dATP → heptameric wheel → recruits and activates **procaspase-9**.

**Step 5 — Executioner Caspase Cascade** (1 mark)
Caspase-9 cleaves and activates **caspase-3 and -7**, which:
- Cleave **PARP** (DNA repair enzyme) → irreversibly shut down repair.
- Cleave **ICAD** → release **CAD** → DNA fragmentation.
- Cleave **lamins, fodrin, gelsolin** → nuclear condensation, membrane blebbing.

**Step 6 — Conclusion** (1 mark)
Apoptotic bodies express **phosphatidylserine** on outer leaflet → recognized and engulfed by macrophages → **no inflammatory response**, distinguishing apoptosis from necrosis.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Why Cancer Cells Resist Apoptosis** (2 marks)
- **Loss of p53 function** (~50% of human cancers) → no Bax/PUMA/Noxa induction.
- **Overexpression of Bcl-2** (e.g., follicular lymphoma — t(14;18) translocation juxtaposes BCL2 to IgH enhancer).
- **Reduced caspase activity** or upregulation of **IAPs/survivin**.
- **Loss of Apaf-1** in some melanomas.

**Step 2 — Specific Examples** (2 marks)
- **Follicular lymphoma:** BCL2 overexpression.
- **Chronic lymphocytic leukemia:** BCL2 dependence (BH3 mimetics effective).
- **p53-deficient tumors:** resistant to DNA-damaging chemotherapy.
- **HPV-induced cervical cancer:** E6 protein degrades p53; E7 inactivates Rb.

**Step 3 — Therapeutic Strategies** (2 marks)
- **BH3 mimetics:** Venetoclax (ABT-199) binds Bcl-2 → reactivates apoptosis in CLL.
- **Death receptor agonists:** TRAIL receptor agonists (in trials).
- **IAP antagonists:** Smac mimetics (e.g., LCL161).
- **p53 reactivators:** APR-246, eprenetapopt (in clinical trials for TP53-mutant MDS).

**Step 4 — Clinical Significance** (1 mark)
Apoptosis-targeting drugs are central to **precision oncology**, and combination therapy (chemotherapy + apoptosis sensitizer) is an active research frontier.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable diagrams, describe the **role of NF-κB signaling in inflammation and immune responses**. How is NF-κB activated through the canonical pathway?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **molecular basis of long-term potentiation (LTP)** in the hippocampus as a model for learning and memory. Highlight the role of NMDA receptors, Ca²⁺, CaMKII, and CREB.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — NF-κB Family and Inhibitor** (1 mark)
- NF-κB family: **p50, p65 (RelA), c-Rel, RelB**.
- Held inactive in cytosol by **IκBα** inhibitor.

**Step 2 — Canonical Pathway Activation** (3 marks)
1. **Stimulus** (TNF-α, IL-1β, LPS) binds receptor.
2. **TRAF adaptor proteins** recruited.
3. **IKK complex** (IKKα, IKKβ, NEMO) phosphorylates IκBα at Ser32/36.
4. **Phosphorylated IκBα** is ubiquitinated (K48-linked) and degraded by 26S proteasome.
5. **NF-κB (p50/p65)** translocates to nucleus.

**Step 3 — Transcriptional Targets** (1.5 marks)
- **Pro-inflammatory:** TNF-α, IL-6, COX-2, iNOS.
- **Anti-apoptotic:** Bcl-xL, c-IAP1/2, A1.
- **Immune regulators:** MHC-I, IL-2.
- **Acute phase:** CRP, serum amyloid A.

**Step 4 — Termination** (1 mark)
- **IκBα** is itself an NF-κB target → new IκBα shuttles NF-κB back to cytosol (negative feedback).
- A20 deubiquitinase turns off signaling.

**Step 5 — Clinical Significance** (0.5 marks)
Dysregulated NF-κB → chronic inflammation, autoimmune disease, and is a driver of oncogenesis (especially in **diffuse large B-cell lymphoma**).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Hebbian Plasticity Principle** (1 mark)
"Neurons that fire together wire together" — coincident activity strengthens synapses.

**Step 2 — NMDA Receptor as a Coincidence Detector** (2 marks)
NMDA-R requires **two simultaneous events**:
- **Glutamate binding** (chemical input)
- **Post-synaptic depolarization** (electrical input) → relieves Mg²⁺ block.
Only then does Ca²⁺ flow in.

**Step 3 — Ca²⁺ Influx and CaMKII** (2 marks)
$$\text{Ca}^{2+} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Binds calmodulin} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Ca}^{2+}/\text{CaM activates CaMKII}$$

CaMKII autophosphorylates at Thr286 → **Ca²⁺-independent activity** (molecular "memory" of the synapse). It phosphorylates AMPA-R subunits → **increases AMPA-R conductance and trafficking to membrane**.

**Step 4 — Early vs Late LTP** (1 mark)
- **Early LTP** (1–3 hours): post-translational modification, no new gene expression.
- **Late LTP** (>3 hours): requires **gene transcription and new protein synthesis** → **CREB-dependent**.

**Step 5 — CREB Pathway** (1 mark)
$$\text{CaMKIV or PKA or MAPK} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CREB-Ser133-P} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CBP recruitment} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Transcription of BDNF, c-fos, Egr-1, Arc}$$

BDNF binds TrkB receptors → further strengthens synapse.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** LTP with long-term memory storage as identical — LTP is a *cellular mechanism* underlying memory *encoding*.
> - **Don't write** "Ca²⁺ activates PKC" and stop — KTU expects explicit mention of **CaMKII autophosphorylation** as the molecular memory switch.
> - In NF-κB answers, **students commonly miss** the **negative feedback** by IκBα resynthesis — this costs 1 mark.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Apoptosis** = programmed cell death via caspases; two pathways — intrinsic (Bcl-2/MOMP/caspase-9) and extrinsic (death receptor/caspase-8).
- ✅ **Executioner caspases:** **3, 6, 7** — cleave PARP, ICAD, lamins.
- ✅ **Bcl-2 is anti-apoptotic; Bax/Bak are pro-apoptotic.**
- ✅ **p53** is the "guardian of the genome" → upregulates Bax, PUMA, NOXA.
- ✅ **Cell cycle:** driven by **Cyclin-CDK** complexes (Cyclin D-CDK4/6 in G1; Cyclin E-CDK2 in G1/S; Cyclin A-CDK2 in S; Cyclin B-CDK1 in M).
- ✅ **Rb-E2F** is the molecular switch for G1/S; **p53-p21** is the brake.
- ✅ **Cyclin D1 overexpression** is oncogenic (parathyroid adenoma, mantle cell lymphoma).
- ✅ **NF-κB canonical activation:** stimulus → IKK → IκBα degradation → NF-κB nuclear entry.
- ✅ **MAPK/ERK → Elk-1 → c-Fos** for growth factor-induced transcription.
- ✅ **JAK-STAT:** cytokine receptors without intrinsic kinase activity → STAT phosphorylation → dimerization → nuclear entry.
- ✅ **Cardiovascular:** β1-AR → Gs → AC → cAMP → PKA → ↑Ca²⁺ → contraction; β-blockers reduce cAMP.
- ✅ **LTP:** NMDA-R coincidence detector → Ca²⁺ → CaMKII (early) → CREB-dependent gene transcription (late).
- ✅ **T-cell signaling:** TCR → ZAP-70 → LAT → PLCγ1 → Ca²⁺ + DAG → NFAT + NF-κB + AP-1.
- ✅ **Cancer drugs often target:** HER2 (Trastuzumab), Bcr-Abl (Imatinib), BRAF (Vemurafenib), BCL-2 (Venetoclax).
- ✅ **Most-tested KTU points:** Caspase cascade, Rb-E2F, p53, NF-κB mechanism, NMDA-R/CREB.
